name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: Neelakantan 2018 (DIO mice, ~/day): ↓body weight, ↓fat mass, ↓adipocyte size, no change in food intake; target-validated by Kraus 2014 (genetic knockdown, Nature)
caveat: Compelling but rodent-only; "no change in food intake" is a genuinely interesting mechanistic point, but it's still animals. No human efficacy

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: Kraus 2014 Nature (knockdown) + human adipose NNMT-expression correlations (Kannt 2015)
caveat: The target is well-validated (including some human correlational data); the drug is not — don't conflate them

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: Neelakantan 2019 (aged mice: stem-cell reactivation, ~70% peak-torque gain)
caveat: Aged-mouse only; intriguing "recomp" angle but no human data

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: Mouse: ~30% lower total cholesterol; Kraus/Brachs: improved insulin sensitivity (glucose tolerance less consistent)
caveat: Preclinical; specific LDL / clinical-lipid effects not established

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: None — no completed human trials, no published PK
caveat: The defining gap: bioavailability, half-life, dosing, safety in humans are all unknown

name: 5-Amino-1MQ (NNMT inhibitor)
base: Short rodent studies: "no observable toxicity" over ~11 days
caveat: Reassuring in mice over days; says nothing about human safety or chronic use

name: Adipotide (FTPP / Prohibitin-TP01)
outcome: Weight loss / fat reduction (the marketed use)
base: Strong preclinical: mice (Nat Med 2004) + obese rhesus monkeys ~11% body weight/28 d (Sci Transl Med 2011)
caveat: Dramatic in animals, but the human trial was terminated for toxicity before any efficacy was established/published

name: Adipotide (FTPP / Prohibitin-TP01)
base: Monkey: ~50% less insulin needed post-treatment
caveat: Preclinical only; never confirmed in humans

name: Adipotide (FTPP / Prohibitin-TP01)
base: Phase 1 (2012), obese prostate-cancer patients — terminated; no published efficacy
caveat: The one human trial ended early on safety; efficacy in humans is unknown

name: Adipotide (FTPP / Prohibitin-TP01)
base: Nephrotoxicity — dose-dependent renal-tubular changes (monkey) → human Phase 1 terminated for kidney toxicity → discontinued 2019
caveat: This is the defining fact: a documented, trial-ending kidney-toxicity signal. Not theoretical — it stopped the program

name: Adipotide (FTPP / Prohibitin-TP01)
base: Prohibitin-homing + apoptotic ablation well-characterised preclinically; spawned "hunter-killer" peptide class
caveat: The biology is validated and influential; the drug failed clinically. Target ≠ safe drug

name: Afamelanotide
outcome: EPP — phototoxicity prevention / pain-free light exposure (the approved use)
base: 3 randomized vehicle-controlled Phase 3 trials (CUV029/030/039, 244 adults) → EMA 2015 + FDA 2019; ~64 vs ~41 pain-free sunlight hours/180 days
caveat: Approved, first-in-class for a genuine unmet need — but for an ultra-rare disease; effect is meaningful-but-modest (extra tolerated-light hours), not a cure; EU approval under exceptional circumstances

name: Afamelanotide
base: Robust, well-characterised pharmacology (MC1R → eumelanogenesis)
caveat: The core mechanism is real and reproducible; it is what the drug does

name: Afamelanotide
base: Mechanistically works (same eumelanin pathway); no approval for this indication
caveat: The pathway works, but it's unapproved for cosmetics, uses an injectable systemic drug for vanity, and gray-market product quality is unverified; approved-disease data don't transfer to cosmetic safety

name: Afamelanotide
outcome: Other explored uses (vitiligo, PLE, solar urticaria, acne)
base: Early Clinuvel trials 2007-2011
caveat: Mostly unpublished or not pursued; vitiligo saw some further work; not established

name: Afamelanotide
base: Not an approved or established use
caveat: The EPP approval is disease-specific; it is not a general "safe tanning / sun-protection" drug

name: Afamelanotide
base: Approval-grade dataset (244 patients + post-marketing)
caveat: Implant-site reactions, nausea, oropharyngeal pain, cough, fatigue, skin hyperpigmentation, dizziness, melanocytic nevi (new/changing moles). A theoretical melanoma risk → full-body skin exam twice yearly is recommended; MC1R-selectivity gives it a cleaner systemic profile than Melanotan II

name: Copper Tripeptide-3
outcome: Hair-follicle elongation / dermal-papilla-cell proliferation (in vitro / ex vivo)
base: Pyo 2007 (Arch Pharm Res) — cultured human follicles + DPCs
caveat: Ex vivo / in vitro only; a single foundational study; a lab effect is not a clinical effect

name: Copper Tripeptide-3
outcome: Topical serum increasing hair density in people (the marketed use)
base: No robust human RCT of an AHK-Cu serum
caveat: The lab signal doesn't establish a clinical serum effect; penetration to the dermal papilla from a serum is doubtful (enhanced-delivery methods like microneedling perform better)

name: Copper Tripeptide-3
base: None (cosmetic)
caveat: Not shown for a cosmetic serum — and it is a drug claim (cf. minoxidil / finasteride)

name: Copper Tripeptide-3
base: Broad cosmetic use
caveat: No hormonal effects (unlike finasteride); suitable for both sexes; long-term data lacking

name: AICAR (Acadesine)
base: Narkar 2008 (Cell): sedentary mice +~44% running endurance, type-I fiber shift
caveat: Landmark — but mouse-only; no human endurance trials; WADA-banned precisely for this

name: AICAR (Acadesine)
base: Well-characterised: AMPK → ACC inactivation, GLUT4, PGC-1α (cells + animals)
caveat: Strong mechanistic/animal data; not established as a human therapeutic

name: AICAR (Acadesine)
base: Legitimate clinical trials in cardiac-surgery ischemia
caveat: Real clinical study history — but did not achieve general approval; not a consumer indication

name: AICAR (Acadesine)
base: Decades of use as the standard AMPK probe
caveat: BUT 2021 review: many effects AMPK-independent → interpret AICAR-only studies cautiously

name: AICAR (Acadesine)
base: Trial data at therapeutic doses
caveat: Trials: transient uric-acid rise, mild lactic acidosis, possible hypoglycemia (insulin-independent glucose uptake); no organ-toxicity signal noted — but gray-market self-injection is unstudied, and it's banned/detectable in sport

name: Alpha Arbutin
base: Competitive tyrosinase inhibition (Boo 2021 mechanistic review); Sugimoto 2004 three-dimensional human-skin model reporting melanin reduced to roughly 40% of control
effect: Gradual fading of dark spots and uneven tone
caveat: Gentle and gradual; daily sun protection essential

name: Alpha Arbutin
base: Consistent tyrosinase mechanism plus accumulating human data
caveat: Most human data comes from combination products, not arbutin alone

name: Alpha Arbutin
base: Tantanasrigul 2024 split-face, evaluator-blinded randomized pilot — a 5% alpha-arbutin + 2% kojic acid cream comparable to a prescription triple-combination cream over 12 weeks, with lower recurrence after stopping
effect: Comparable melasma improvement to a prescription combination in one pilot
caveat: A combination result (arbutin plus kojic), not arbutin alone; a single small pilot

name: Alpha Arbutin
outcome: Isolated standalone efficacy (arbutin alone vs control)
base: Most human trials are combinations; standalone clinical data is thinner
caveat: Hard to isolate arbutin's own contribution from its formulation partners

name: Alpha Arbutin
outcome: Gentleness & safety (no free hydroquinone at cosmetic concentration)
base: EU-SCCS recognises up to 2% on the face as safe; non-cytotoxic at cosmetic strengths, with no hydroquinone-type ochronosis or rebound reported
effect: A gentle, well-tolerated entry point among brighteners
caveat: A recognised advantage, not an efficacy grade; patch test a new product

name: Amycretin
base: Phase 1b/2a (Lancet 2025, → −24.3% at 36 wk)
caveat: Early-phase (Phase 1b/2a), small; needs Phase 3 confirmation; high trial withdrawal

name: Amycretin
base: Phase 1 (Lancet 2025, ~13.1% at 12 wk, > semaglutide's ~6%)
caveat: Early, short (12 wk); Phase 3 pending

name: Amycretin
base: Phase 2 (n=448, up to −1.8%)
caveat: Phase 2; not approved

name: Amycretin
base: Phase 1/2 (GI-predominant, mostly mild-moderate)
caveat: GI events frequent (as with GLP-1/amylin); long-term profile unestablished; investigational

name: AOD-9604 (hGH fragment 176-191 analog)
base: Pivotal Phase 2b (~500+ obese adults, 12 wk, placebo-controlled): did NOT meet primary endpoint; effect modest, not clinically meaningful → development abandoned 2007
caveat: The strongest kind of "F" — tested in a proper trial and failed. Not a data gap; a negative result

name: AOD-9604 (hGH fragment 176-191 analog)
base: Animal + in-vitro: stimulates fat breakdown, inhibits lipogenesis (receptor-independent)
caveat: Real in the lab / animals — but the lab effect did not translate into human weight loss (that's the whole lesson)

name: AOD-9604 (hGH fragment 176-191 analog)
base: Human trials: no IGF-1 elevation, no glucose disturbance
caveat: Here the molecule genuinely delivered — it is metabolically clean; unfortunately "clean but ineffective" doesn't make a drug

name: AOD-9604 (hGH fragment 176-191 analog)
base: Explored post-obesity; some interest
caveat: Not established; a second unproven indication

name: AOD-9604 (hGH fragment 176-191 analog)
base: 6 trials, 900+ participants
caveat: Well-characterised, well-tolerated (this is genuinely its strong suit) — but safety without efficacy isn't a therapy; and it's FDA-flagged as a bulk substance + WADA-banned

name: Acetyl Hexapeptide-8
base: Small human studies — Blanes-Mira 2002; Wang 2013 (~30% wrinkle-depth reduction at 10%)
caveat: Mixed: strongest positives manufacturer-funded; an independent 2023 study found no significant reduction; small samples

name: Acetyl Hexapeptide-8
outcome: Reaching the neuromuscular junction at cosmetic doses (the "muscle-relaxing" premise)
base: Penetration data
caveat: Peptide must cross the stratum corneum to reach the NMJ; available data suggest it largely does not at cosmetic concentrations

name: Acetyl Hexapeptide-8
base: None (no head-to-head)
caveat: No trial compares it to botulinum toxin; mechanism is fundamentally different (reversible competition vs enzymatic cleavage); marketing overstates the evidence

name: Acetyl Hexapeptide-8
base: CIR Expert Panel assessment; broad cosmetic use
caveat: Regarded as safe as used; suitable for sensitive skin

name: Azelaic Acid
base: King 2023 systematic review (43 RCTs) — azelaic more effective than vehicle; comparable to topical retinoids on inflammatory lesions in review-level evidence
effect: Reduced appearance of breakouts and post-acne marks
caveat: Strongest data at prescription 15–20%; cosmetic-accessible form is milder; acne is a medical condition

name: Azelaic Acid
base: King 2023 — 15% gel comparable to (or better than) metronidazole in review-level evidence
caveat: The prescription 15% gel holds the strongest data; rosacea is a condition for a dermatologist

name: Azelaic Acid
base: King 2023; selective tyrosinase inhibition of overactive melanocytes, fading a spot without lightening surrounding skin
effect: Gradual fading of dark spots and post-inflammatory marks
caveat: Gradual; formulation-dependent; daily sun protection essential

name: Azelaic Acid
base: King 2023 + Albzea 2023 meta-analysis — 20% azelaic comparable to 4% hydroquinone and better than 2% hydroquinone; lower melasma severity index than hydroquinone across pooled trials
effect: Visible evening of melasma-related pigmentation over weeks to months
caveat: Strongest at 20% (prescription); melasma recurs and needs a dermatologist for stubborn cases

name: Azelaic Acid
base: King 2023 found no eligible RCTs for skin-aging
caveat: No controlled evidence supports an anti-wrinkle claim

name: Azelaic Acid
base: Marketing claim
caveat: No human study has compared a derivative head-to-head against azelaic acid itself, so the equivalence has never been tested. The 43 pooled RCTs and the melasma meta-analysis enrolled azelaic acid at 15–20% with no derivative arm; the one lower-strength comparison varied the vehicle, not the molecule. Relabelling to a derivative is an honest ingredient name — inheriting the azelaic trial record is not.

name: Azelaic Acid
base: Broad cosmetic and prescription use; well tolerated
effect: Generally well tolerated; widely regarded as often compatible with pregnancy
caveat: Mild, transient tingling is common early; patch test. Pregnancy compatibility is a doctor's call to confirm, not medical clearance

name: Bakuchiol
base: Dhaliwal 2019 (Br J Dermatol) — randomised, double-blind, 12-week trial n=44, bakuchiol 0.5% cream twice daily vs retinol 0.5% cream once daily; both significantly decreased wrinkle surface area with no statistical difference between compounds. Draelos 2020 (JDD) — separate clinical evaluation of nature-based bakuchiol moisturizer in sensitive-skin population.
effect: Visible reduction of wrinkle surface area over 12 weeks, comparable magnitude to 0.5% retinol
caveat: Single independent RCT (n=44 is small); no large multi-site replication or long-term (>12 weeks) trial. Formulation-dependent (0.5% appears to be the studied effective concentration).

name: Bakuchiol
base: Dhaliwal 2019 — significant decrease in hyperpigmentation, no statistical difference vs retinol 0.5%; graded blinded by board-certified dermatologist and by computer photograph analysis.
effect: Visible reduction in appearance of uneven pigmentation over 12 weeks
caveat: Same evidence base as wrinkle outcome (single RCT); replication needed

name: Bakuchiol
base: Dhaliwal 2019 — retinol group reported significantly more facial scaling and stinging; bakuchiol group had lower rates of subjective itching and burning (not statistically significant) and significantly lower stinging
effect: Better tolerability than retinol at equivalent concentration; suitable for sensitive skin, no reported "retinization" period
caveat: Studied population was mixed adults with photodamage; specific data on rosacea, active eczema, or highly reactive skin limited

name: Bakuchiol
base: Chaudhuri & Bojanowski 2014 (Int J Cosmet Sci) — comparative gene expression profiling of bakuchiol vs retinol in EpiDermFT full-thickness skin substitutes; volcano plots showed great overall similarity of gene expression modulation. In human dermal fibroblasts, bakuchiol upregulated type I collagen ~1.4-fold and type IV collagen ~1.6-fold, comparable to 0.025% retinol. Mechanism involves MAPK/ERK signalling — not RAR/RXR receptor binding (bakuchiol has no structural resemblance to retinoids).
effect: Retinol-like gene expression signature and collagen upregulation via non-retinoid pathway
caveat: Chaudhuri & Bojanowski are Sytheon Ltd employees (Sytenol A patent holder) — declared conflict of interest. Independent replication of full gene profile is limited. Mechanism is real but paper is commercially motivated.

name: Bakuchiol
base: In vitro: bakuchiol ~60-fold more effective than natural tocopherol at inhibiting squalene peroxidation (IC50 0.5 vs 30 µg/mL); reduces UVB- induced IL-6 and TNF-α in keratinocyte cultures ~35–45%
effect: Antioxidant activity in skin cells and lipids
caveat: Cell-culture data; clinical translation to visible endpoints in humans not directly demonstrated at this granularity

name: Bakuchiol
base: Limited clinical trials at 0.5–1% in acne-affected skin; comprehensive review 2020 (JOID) discusses evidence but base is small
effect: Reduction in appearance of blemishes and pore appearance
caveat: Acne treatment is medical territory (retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, antibiotics); cosmetic scope covers appearance only. Evidence base smaller than for photoaging.

name: Bakuchiol
base: Marketing claim
caveat: A compositional claim, not a clinical one — no human study can bear on it. Bakuchiol is a meroterpene phenol (C22H34O2); retinol is C20H30O. It contains no retinol, is not a retinoid, and does not bind retinoic acid receptors. The only support ever offered is a retinol-like gene-expression signature in full-thickness skin substitutes and cultured dermal fibroblasts — in vitro, and authored by employees of the ingredient's patent holder. Matching retinol in a trial does not make a molecule retinol.

name: Bakuchiol
base: Marketing claim
caveat: A real head-to-head was run: randomised, double-blind, 12 weeks, n=44, 0.5% bakuchiol against 0.5% retinol, independent of the ingredient supplier. Both arms significantly reduced wrinkle surface area and hyperpigmentation with no statistical difference between them, graded blind by a board-certified dermatologist and by computer photograph analysis; further human work in sensitive skin and an independent replication point the same way. The evidence is still early and small — one independent trial of that size over that span, designed to detect superiority with no prespecified non-inferiority margin, so a difference not detected is not equivalence demonstrated.

name: Bakuchiol
base: Bakuchiol is not a retinoid (structurally unrelated) and does not carry the vitamin A cumulative exposure concern that led to EU retinol restrictions (Regulation 2024/996). However — no dedicated pregnancy safety studies exist; FDA has not assigned a pregnancy safety rating.
effect: Widely marketed as pregnancy-safe retinol alternative; not classified as a pregnancy risk by regulatory authorities
caveat: Absence of evidence of risk is not evidence of absence of risk. Pregnant and breastfeeding users should consult a healthcare provider before starting any new active. Vallydia does not make pregnancy-specific claims and directs users to their doctor for individual assessment.

name: Bakuchiol
base: To carry the INCI name "Bakuchiol", the ingredient must be >80% pure. Lower-purity Psoralea corylifolia (babchi) seed extracts and oils may contain furocoumarins (psoralen, isopsoralen) that are photosensitising and can cause serious skin reactions upon UV exposure. These are not bakuchiol per se — they are contaminants of unpurified extract.
effect: Well-purified bakuchiol has a clean tolerability profile; unpurified babchi extracts carry meaningfully different safety concerns
caveat: When formulating or evaluating a product, verify the INCI declaration shows "Bakuchiol" (purified) and not "Psoralea corylifolia extract" or "babchi oil" — the latter are not equivalent for safety purposes

name: Bakuchiol
base: Some traditional-medicine and animal studies of Psoralea corylifolia extract
effect: Not applicable — Vallydia treats bakuchiol strictly as a topical cosmetic ingredient
caveat: Oral use of babchi extracts is associated with hepatotoxicity concerns in some case reports (Chinese herbal medicine context) and is outside cosmetic scope entirely

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
base: Phase 2 RCT (Heymsfield 2021; fat ~−20%, lean ~+3–4%, insulin sensitivity ↑)
caveat: Real human Phase 2, but not approved; single-agent weight change is near-neutral

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
outcome: Muscle-sparing + fat loss with GLP-1 (the thesis)
base: BELIEVE Phase 2b + semaglutide (Nature Med 2026; >90% of loss as fat, lean preserved)
caveat: Emerging and positive, but Phase 2b; tirzepatide combos mixed (one terminated)

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
base: Phase 2b/3 (RESILIENT)
caveat: Grew muscle but did not improve function — the "mass ≠ function" wall

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
base: Phase 2
caveat: Same mass-vs-function caveat

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
base: Phase 2 (~18% improvement)
caveat: Secondary; consistent

name: Bimagrumab (BYM338)
base: Phase 2 across indications
caveat: Muscle cramps/spasms, diarrhea, mild pancreatic-enzyme rises, acne; relatively clean vs older ActRII traps

name: BPC-157
outcome: GI / gastric mucosal protection (NSAID-, ethanol-, stress-induced ulcers)
base: Numerous rodent studies since 1993; the most-replicated finding
caveat: Robust preclinical signal only; no completed human RCT

name: BPC-157
base: Multiple rodent models incl. transected Achilles; independent replication (Taiwan)
caveat: Consistent in animals; mechanism plausible; no human efficacy trial

name: BPC-157
base: Rodent crush/transection/fracture models
caveat: Preclinical only; heterogeneous designs

name: BPC-157
base: Early rodent models
caveat: Far from human evidence

name: BPC-157
base: Preclinical + limited
caveat: FDA's July-2026 briefing proposed not listing it; inadequate human data

name: BPC-157
outcome: Marketed "healing stack" / human recovery, performance, anti-aging
base: None (extrapolated from animal data)
caveat: No controlled human trials support these marketing claims

name: BPC-157
base: No adequate human data
caveat: Long-term safety not established; gray-market purity/sterility is a separate risk

name: Cagrilintide
base: Phase 3 REDEFINE-1/-2 (~20–23%); NDA filed Dec 2025
caveat: Strong Phase 3 and FDA-filed, but not yet approved; the result disappointed inflated expectations

name: Cagrilintide
base: Phase 2 (Lancet 2021, ~10.8%) + REDEFINE arm (~11.8%)
caveat: Not being filed standalone; lower nausea than GLP-1

name: Cagrilintide
base: Established amylin biology
caveat: Mechanistically distinct from GLP-1

name: Cagrilintide
base: REDEFINE programme
caveat: Combination GI effects are additive; serious events (pancreatitis, gallbladder) similar to semaglutide; investigational

name: Ceramides
base: Multiple RCTs and controlled trials; multicenter study n=312 (4-week ceramide-enriched regimen, SCORAD −61.2%); 2019 qualitative review of 12 clinical studies; 2024-2025 controlled trials showing structurally skin-identical ceramide NP/AP/EOP formulations reduce transepidermal water loss and raise stratum corneum lipid content
effect: Measurable reduction in transepidermal water loss and improved skin hydration, typically within 1–2 weeks of consistent twice-daily use
caveat: Efficacy depends heavily on formulation — ceramides work as barrier lipids only when combined with cholesterol and free fatty acids in balanced ratios (see mechanism). Isolated ceramides in a poor vehicle underperform.

name: Ceramides
outcome: Adjunct in atopic dermatitis / eczema-prone dry skin (appearance & comfort)
base: Multiple interventional studies in mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis; ceramide-dominant formulations achieve ~45–60% clinical success (IGA clear/almost clear) in 3–8 week studies; physiological-lipid formulations (MVE+GL) outperform simple emollients in barrier integrity
effect: Reduced dryness, itch, and roughness; improved barrier integrity as an adjunct to (not replacement for) medical treatment
caveat: Atopic dermatitis is a medical condition — ceramide cosmetics are barrier-supportive adjuncts, not treatments. Persistent or severe cases need a dermatologist.

name: Ceramides
outcome: Aged / menopausal skin barrier (hydration & smoothness appearance)
base: Clinical research in elderly individuals showing high-concentration ceramide formulations improve hydration and barrier function; consistent with the age-related decline in endogenous ceramide content
effect: Improved skin hydration and the appearance of smoother skin
caveat: Fewer large trials specific to aged skin than to atopic dermatitis; effect is barrier/hydration-mediated, not collagen-building

name: Ceramides
base: Small open-label studies (e.g. rice-derived ceramide supplementation n=50) suggesting hydration benefit; evidence far thinner than for topical
caveat: Oral use is outside cosmetic scope and much less established than topical. Not relevant to Vallydia's sellable topical products.

name: Cerebrolysin
base: Multiple RCTs + 2020 systematic review (J Neural Transm); often cited as its strongest indication
caveat: Among the better-supported uses — but Cochrane still cautious; manufacturer funding pervasive

name: Cerebrolysin
base: Meta-analysis of 6 double-blind placebo-controlled trials: significant vs placebo at 4 wk, effect size ~ cholinesterase inhibitors
caveat: Positive signal, but short follow-up, small trials, industry-sponsored; Cochrane "very low" quality

name: Cerebrolysin
base: CASTA (1,070 pts): improved 90-day cognition; several rehab RCTs
caveat: Individual trials positive; 2023 Cochrane: no mortality benefit + ↑ serious AEs (RR ~2.39); 2012 trial doubted benefit except severe cases

name: Cerebrolysin
base: RCTs + 2025 mechanistic work on secondary-injury modulation
caveat: Suggestive; smaller/heterogeneous evidence

name: Cerebrolysin
outcome: Nootropic use in healthy adults (the gray-market use)
base: Essentially none — trials are in disease populations
caveat: No evidence it helps healthy cognition or prevents dementia; extrapolation from patient data is unjustified

name: Cerebrolysin
base: Three decades of Cochrane appraisal
caveat: The core problem: predominantly manufacturer-funded, high risk of bias, no independent confirmation. Large ≠ high-quality

name: Cerebrolysin
base: Large exposure across trials/clinical use
caveat: Generally described as safe short-term; porcine allergy = absolute contraindication; rare seizure reports (causality unclear); caution with serotonergic antidepressants; 2023 Cochrane flagged ↑ non-fatal serious AEs

name: CJC-1295
base: Phase 1 (Teichman 2006) + animal studies
caveat: Pharmacology is real; but raising GH/IGF-1 ≠ a proven clinical benefit

name: CJC-1295
base: Phase 2 (NCT00267527) — halted
caveat: Trial terminated after a participant death; no efficacy conclusion reached

name: CJC-1295
base: None in humans
caveat: Extrapolated from GH/IGF-1 changes; never tested for these goals

name: CJC-1295
base: Limited; a trial-death shadow
caveat: Causation disputed; GH/IGF-1 elevation carries theoretical risks (IGF-1/oncologic, insulin resistance, edema); WADA-banned

name: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin ("CJC/Ipa")
base: Mechanistic (GHRH + GHRP synergy is established); each component raises GH in humans
effect: Larger GH pulse than either alone (plausible)
caveat: Mechanistically sound, but see below — the specific combo is essentially untested in controlled trials

name: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin ("CJC/Ipa")
outcome: The specific CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin combination for any clinical endpoint
base: No controlled clinical trial has evaluated it
caveat: Zero combination RCT; anti-aging/body-composition claims are extrapolation

name: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin ("CJC/Ipa")
base: No dedicated human safety data
caveat: Inherits both components' unknowns; GH/IGF-1 elevation theoretical risks; WADA-banned

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: Hickson 2019 (diabetic kidney disease): D+Q measurably reduced senescent cells in people
caveat: The genuine landmark — first human senolytic clearance; but clearance ≠ clinical benefit

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: D+Q and fisetin extend healthspan/lifespan across mouse models; fisetin top of 10 flavonoids
caveat: Strong mouse data — the usual "mouse ≠ human" gap applies

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: SToMP-AD Phase 1: "very few significant results, no suggestion of effectiveness"; STAMINA pilot (n=12) feasibility only
caveat: Tested — no cognitive benefit shown; safety/feasibility only

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: Mayo Phase 2: missed on whole group; burden-dependent subgroup signal
caveat: Negative primary; hypothesis that baseline senescent burden dictates response

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: Fisetin AFFIRM-LITE: mixed, some positive physical-function trends
caveat: Preliminary/mixed; ongoing

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
base: Short "hit-and-run" pulses safe/well-tolerated in trials to date
caveat: Flavonoids benign; dasatinib is a real chemo drug — off-label self-use is hazardous

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
base: Preclinical only: rodent cognitive-restoration (scopolamine/aged) + slice synaptogenesis; "10M× BDNF" claim
caveat: Zero human data; the headline potency claim traces to retracted, fabricated work

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
base: Original binding paper (Kd 65 pM) retracted 2025; some independent HGF/c-Met corroboration (Sun 2021)
caveat: Best treated as an unproven working hypothesis, not established fact

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
base: The related drug fosgonimeton failed ACT-AD + LIFT-AD (no better than placebo)
caveat: The one clinical program on this pathway/lineage failed — a strong negative signal

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
base: None
caveat: No trials, no safety profile, very long duration → adverse effects not rapidly reversible

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
base: Pathway-level concern
caveat: HGF/c-Met is a validated cancer-driver pathway; chronically potentiating it for enhancement is a real theoretical risk, unquantified in humans

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
base: Monnier & Schoenenberger 1977 (rabbit)
caveat: The original finding; animal, and even here later work was mixed

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
base: Small 1980s studies (Schneider-Helmert) + Bes et al. 1992
effect: Mixed; some positive, but the strongest trial showed only weak effects
caveat: Human evidence is limited and contradictory — the "sleep-inducing" name overpromises

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
base: Older, small studies
caveat: Thin; not replicated to modern standards

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
caveat: No confirmed receptor; gene not identified; possible bacterial origin

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
base: No modern human safety RCT
caveat: Long-term safety not established

name: Ectoin
base: Multiple placebo-controlled clinical trials over 20+ years; consistent TEWL reductions of 15–72% depending on skin state and formulation
effect: Measurable reduction in transepidermal water loss; barrier stabilisation
caveat: Effect size varies with vehicle and baseline barrier state; well-established but not head-to-head vs ceramide-based formulas in most trials

name: Ectoin
base: Controlled trials with 0.5–7% ectoin creams and lotions
effect: Measurable increase in stratum corneum hydration; sustained over weeks of use
caveat: Effect works via osmolyte mechanism (water-shell around cells) rather than humectant (like HA)

name: Ectoin
outcome: Redness and appearance of sensitive/reactive skin
base: Controlled trials showing measurable reduction in visible erythema and subjective irritation scores; use in sensitive-skin adjacent categories
effect: Reduction in appearance of redness (~38% in some trials)
caveat: Adjunct effect; not a treatment for underlying skin conditions

name: Ectoin
base: Multiple clinical trials with 5.5–7% ectoin cream (branded medical device products); comparable to low-potency corticosteroid in some studies
caveat: Medical device territory in EU, not cosmetic; do not conflate medical claims with topical cosmetic serum framing

name: Ectoin
outcome: Protection against UV and pollution-induced skin stress
base: In-vitro on skin cells and controlled human studies with UV/pollution exposure models
effect: Measurable protection of cell viability and reduction of oxidative markers
caveat: Not a sunscreen; adjunct to UV protection, not replacement

name: Ectoin
base: Limited direct evidence; effect largely inferred from barrier/hydration benefits
caveat: Ectoin protects and hydrates; it does not build collagen or replace retinol. Framing as an anti-aging hero is a marketing overstatement.

name: Ectoin
outcome: "Repairs the skin barrier" lasting structural repair
base: Marketing claim
caveat: Repeated placebo-controlled trials and an independent systematic review test this exact endpoint, and the direction is right — transepidermal water loss falls, barrier function stabilises. It falls short of repair: the effect holds only while the product is applied, and no trial shows structural restoration persisting after use stops. The osmolyte water shell steadies the barrier; it does not rebuild it.

name: Ectoin
base: Decades of cosmetic use; extensive tolerability data
caveat: Hypoallergenic, pregnancy-friendly, no known conflicts with other cosmetic actives; suitable for infants and reactive skin

name: Eloralintide
base: Phase 2, Lancet 2025 (263 pts, 48 wk): up to 20.1% (−21.3 kg) vs 0.4% placebo; dose-dependent; Phase 1 ~11%/12 wk
caveat: Standout Phase 2 — highest amylin-monotherapy weight loss reported — but not yet Phase 3-confirmed; predominantly female cohort (78%)

name: Eloralintide
base: GI + fatigue AEs, dose-dependent; similar to placebo at; slower escalation helped
caveat: Strong — but the highest-efficacy (highest-dose) arms had more AEs; escalation protocol matters

name: Eloralintide
base: Improvements in waist, BP, lipids, glycemia, inflammation
caveat: Consistent secondary benefits; Phase 2 level

name: Eloralintide
base: Trial ongoing (LY3841136 + tirzepatide)
caveat: No combination efficacy data yet

name: Eloralintide
base: Phase 2 (263) + Phase 1
caveat: No unexpected signals; investigational — Phase 3/long-term safety pending

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
base: Human-cell studies — Khavinson 2003; a 2025 independent replication reported dose-dependent extension
caveat: Foundational data dominated by one group; the quantitative pathway was only recently tested independently

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
base: Drosophila, mice/rats — largely the Khavinson/Anisimov group
caveat: Overwhelmingly single-group; independent replication limited

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
base: Older Russian human studies
caveat: Methodologically limited; not independently replicated to modern RCT standards

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
base: Older Russian studies
caveat: Weak, non-replicated

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
outcome: Marketed "reverses aging / turns back the cellular clock"
base: None rigorous
caveat: Telomerase-in-cells ≠ proven human longevity; the extrapolation is unsupported

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
base: No modern human safety data
caveat: Telomerase activation carries a theoretical oncologic concern; long-term safety not established

name: Exosomes
outcome: Topical antioxidant / soothing / barrier support (plant-derived cosmetic exosomes)
base: Mechanistically plausible — plant-derived exosomes carry antioxidant, vitamin, and anti-inflammatory cargo and are good nano-delivery vehicles — but the supporting data is mostly in-vitro (lab cells) or animal, with only small early human work.
effect: Plausible, modest antioxidant and soothing/barrier support as a supporting cosmetic ingredient
caveat: Most plant-exosome evidence is lab- or animal-based rather than human topical trials; benefits are modest and supportive, not transformative.

name: Exosomes
outcome: Short-term skin-appearance benefits (exosomes broadly — hydration, elasticity, fine lines, pores, pigmentation)
base: A 2026 systematic review pooling 19 human studies found short-term associations with improved hydration, elasticity, wrinkles, pores, and pigmentation.
caveat: Most studies were not randomised, methods were heterogeneous, and follow-up was limited — the review framed the effects as early and not yet confirmed. Much of this signal comes from injected or in-office MSC (stem-cell) exosomes in clinical settings, which does not transfer to a plant exosome in a cosmetic cream.

name: Exosomes
outcome: "Regenerative" skin transformation from a topical (especially plant) exosome
base: No topical evidence; regenerative signalling is the claimed role of human/stem-cell exosomes used clinically (often injected).
effect: Not supported for a plant/topical cosmetic
caveat: Plant exosomes act via antioxidant delivery and soothing, not cellular regeneration. Any product implying a topical plant exosome will "regenerate" skin borrows from the human clinical/injected lane and overstates the evidence.

name: Exosomes
outcome: Established anti-ageing category (the field as a whole)
base: A 2024 PRISMA systematic review of regenerative aesthetics (covering exosomes alongside stem cells and PDRN).
caveat: Despite some well-designed trials, the review found a prevalent gap in molecular and clinical evidence and concluded the field lacks the scientific rigour and regulatory compliance to be considered established.

name: Exosomes
outcome: Injected / clinical (MSC) exosome therapy — for context, NOT cosmetic scope
base: Human/stem-cell exosomes are studied in clinical and in-office (often injected or post-procedure) settings, where more of the exosome evidence sits.
effect: A separate, regulated, clinical lane — not a cosmetic topical
caveat: As of 2025 the FDA had NOT approved any exosome product for therapeutic use outside clinical trials; human-derived exosomes trigger the HCT/P (human cells/tissues) framework, and injected use carries procedural risks. Out of scope for a cosmetic reference and not what a topical serum delivers.

name: Exosomes
base: Marketing claim
caveat: No exosome product is FDA-approved for therapeutic use outside clinical trials, and cosmetic ingredients have no FDA approval pathway at all, so there is no such approval to have been granted. Falling under the HCT/P framework is a regulatory classification, not an approval. This is a claim about regulatory status, not about skin — the human exosome data cited here measures skin appearance, not approval status.

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
outcome: Muscle hypertrophy via myostatin inhibition (the biology)
base: Transgenic + gene-therapy animals (20–40%, more in overexpression); Lee 2007
caveat: This is the biology in transgenic / gene-therapy models — not injected protein in humans

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
outcome: Injected follistatin protein → muscle in healthy humans (the marketed use)
base: None
caveat: No human clinical evidence; the animal gains were transgenic / gene-therapy

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
outcome: Gene therapy for muscular dystrophy / IBM
base: Phase 1/2 (Mendell)
caveat: Well tolerated locally, but modest; never became an approved therapy

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
base: Antibodies (MYO-029 etc.) + ACE-031
caveat: Antibodies: modest/no functional benefit; ACE-031 terminated on vascular safety

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
base: Preclinical + gene-therapy + mechanism
caveat: Suppresses FSH/LH (reproductive); vascular risk (class); may lower bone density (GDF-11 block); muscle may outpace tendon; theoretical oncogenic concern; effects may not "cycle off"; gray-market product often mislabelled/contaminated; WADA-banned

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
base: Phase 2/3 LIFT-AD: missed primary (GST) + key secondaries; ACT-AD Ph2 missed; earlier failures
caveat: Tested and failed at pivotal scale — cognition/function no better than placebo (P≈0.70). Not "untested" — clearly negative

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
base: SHAPE cut 75→28 patients, missed primary; "5-patient subgroup"
caveat: Underpowered, failed; subgroup grasping

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
base: Post-hoc / prespecified subgroups after failed primaries
caveat: Classic post-hoc rescue; hypothesis-generating at best, not evidence of efficacy

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
base: Real: MET signaling diminished in AD hippocampus; neuroprotective in models
caveat: The biology is legitimate; it didn't translate to clinical benefit — the whole point

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
base: Phase 1–3 exposure
caveat: Consistently safe and well-tolerated — its failure was efficacy, not safety

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: Landmark Cell 2017 + repeated validation (Nat Commun 2025, etc.): selective apoptosis of senescent cells (reported 30–70% reduction across tissues)
caveat: Genuinely elegant, reproducible preclinical senolysis — one of the few peptides to cross from in-vitro to in-vivo — but still animal/cell only

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: Cell 2017: restored fur, kidney function, physical fitness; ~24.8% median-lifespan extension
caveat: Striking mouse result; no human data at all

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: Comms Biol 2025; Aging 2020; Cell 2017 (doxorubicin recovery)
caveat: Interesting mechanism-extensions, all preclinical

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: None — no Phase 1 ever conducted
caveat: The defining gap: human effects completely unknown

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: Co-IP, fluorescence, 2025 structural work confirm selective p53-FOXO4 disruption
caveat: The biology is well-demonstrated and beautiful; that's not the same as a safe human drug

name: FOXO4-DRI
base: 10-month mouse dosing "no obvious side effects"
caveat: Reassuring in mice — but it manipulates p53 systemically; long-term human oncologic/apoptotic consequences are unstudied

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
outcome: Topical: appearance of photoaged skin (fine lines, firmness, density)
base: Small human RCTs — Leyden 2002 (n=71), eye-cream (n=41), Badenhorst 2016 (nano-carrier vs Matrixyl 3000)
caveat: Small samples; effect generally smaller than prescription retinoids; highly formulation-dependent

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: In-vitro fibroblast studies (Maquart 1988; Pickart)
caveat: In vitro; in-vivo relevance limited by skin penetration

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: Permeation study (Li 2015)
caveat: Key limitation; most benefit shown with enhanced delivery or penetration-optimised formulas

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
outcome: Post-procedure wound healing (e.g., after laser resurfacing)
base: Small controlled trials + animal models
caveat: Medicinal context — not a cosmetic-product claim

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: Mechanistic + small studies
caveat: Weak human evidence

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: In-vitro / bioinformatic (Connectivity Map, ~4,000 genes toward a "younger" profile)
caveat: Bioinformatic/in vitro only; no human systemic-efficacy trials; "reverses aging" is not demonstrated

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The human trials scored appearance endpoints — fine lines, firmness, the look of density — not dermal collagen; no biopsy or histology has measured collagen in skin after topical application. Synthesis of collagen and elastin was shown in fibroblast culture, and permeation through intact skin is near-zero.

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
base: Widely used cosmetic; small trials
caveat: Topical generally well tolerated (occasional mild redness); systemic/injectable human safety not established

name: GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin)
base: Approved diagnostic (Japan) + ~4 human trials (~170)
caveat: Real but narrow; diagnostic-focused; single company

name: GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin)
base: Kaken trials; accuracy competitive with the insulin-tolerance test
caveat: Approved diagnostic use only

name: GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin)
base: None in humans
caveat: Untested for these goals

name: GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin)
base: Diagnostic use + PK data
caveat: Raises cortisol/prolactin (moderate) + appetite; long-term unknown; WADA-banned

name: GHRP-6
base: Extensive characterisation (Bowers 1984 onward) + human PK
caveat: Real, foundational pharmacology

name: GHRP-6
base: Preclinical + Phase 1 observations
caveat: A feature for cachexia, a drawback for GH/fat-loss goals; preclinical as a therapy

name: GHRP-6
outcome: Anti-aging / body composition / muscle (the popular use)
base: None in humans
caveat: Untested for these goals

name: GHRP-6
base: Research + PK data
caveat: Strong hunger; raises cortisol (~15–20% transient) and prolactin; long-term unknown; WADA-banned

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: Foundational, non-controversial cell biology
caveat: Grades GSH's natural role — NOT proof that supplementation delivers cosmetic benefits

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: Some RCT/clinical data (often with vitamin C); modest, inconsistent; low bioavailability
caveat: Mixed results; bioavailability doubts; effect (if any) is subtle and reversible

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: Local depigmentation data
caveat: Effect limited to application site; not systemic

name: Glutathione (GSH)
outcome: IV glutathione — skin lightening (the most popular use)
base: No published trials proving efficacy; unapproved
caveat: Popular but essentially unproven; Philippine FDA: no evidence, no dosing standard

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: One oral study noted possible wrinkle/elasticity benefit + good tolerance
caveat: Preliminary; not established

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: GRAS; generally well-tolerated
caveat: The reassuring part: oral/topical is broadly safe

name: Glutathione (GSH)
base: Health-authority advisories
caveat: Renal/hepatic/thyroid dysfunction, nervous-system effects, Stevens-Johnson syndrome; infection risk (HIV/hepatitis) if non-sterile; IV+vitC → kidney-stone risk; theoretical skin-cancer risk (less eumelanin = less UV protection). Narrow effective-vs-toxic window

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
base: Most potent GHRP (~10× GHRP-6); human PK
caveat: Real, potent pharmacology; GH response desensitises with continuous use

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
base: Preclinical (infarct ↓40–60%, LV function ↑, anti-fibrotic; CD36-KO abolishes it) + incomplete Phase 2
caveat: Preclinical / early; Phase 2 didn't complete; not an established therapy

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
base: Preclinical (Rodrigue-Way 2007)
caveat: Preclinical

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
base: None in humans
caveat: Untested for these goals

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
base: Research + PK data
caveat: Raises cortisol/prolactin; GH desensitisation; WADA-banned

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: Multiple human observational/epidemiological datasets (USC, Barzilai): humanin ↓ with age, ↓ in AD/CAD, ↑ in centenarians + their offspring; correlates across mammalian species
caveat: This is the strongest human evidence — but it's a biomarker correlation, not proof that giving humanin helps. Human association studies are even called "equivocal" in some reviews. Biomarker ≠ drug

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: Rich preclinical: blocks amyloid-beta toxicity, prevents tau hyperphosphorylation, anti-apoptotic in neuron/animal models (mostly HNG)
caveat: Powerful mechanism, zero completed human trials; no RCT shows exogenous humanin prevents or treats AD. Founding story ≠ clinical proof

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: Rodent clamp studies (Muzumdar 2009 — central STAT3-mediated; HNGF6A analog lowered glucose in diabetic rats)
caveat: Preclinical rodent only, small n; CNS-mediated mechanism; not shown in humans

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: Animal: ischemia-reperfusion protection; 14-month HNG dosing prevented age-related myocardial fibrosis in aged mice
caveat: Animal-only; native-vs-HNG gap applies

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: Transgenic worms + multiple mammalian species (levels track lifespan); HNG improved healthspan markers, lowered inflammation in mice
caveat: The headline longevity-therapeutic claim is animal + biomarker, not human-interventional. This is what most buyers want — and it's unproven in people

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
base: No completed human trials → no human safety dataset
caveat: Endogenous peptide (present in everyone), which is reassuring mechanistically, but injected exogenous safety/PK is simply not characterised in humans

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
base: Multiple placebo-controlled trials at 0.1% across MW range (50–2000 kDa); 2024 JCAD multi-weight formulation study; consistent effect across MW
effect: Measurable immediate and sustained increase in skin hydration (corneometry); onset within 30 minutes
caveat: Effect is humectant and surface-focused; depth depends on molecular weight and formulation

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: Fine lines and wrinkle appearance (LMW-HA 50–130 kDa)
base: Pavicic 2011 RCT (n=76, periocular, 60 days, 5 MW compared); LMW-HA showed significant wrinkle depth reduction
effect: Visible reduction of wrinkle depth after 4–8 weeks with low-molecular- weight formulations
caveat: Effect smaller than retinoids or injectables; multi-weight formulations combine surface hydration with deeper penetration

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: Skin elasticity and appearance of firmness
base: Pavicic 2011; multi-weight formulation trials
caveat: Modest effect; depends on formulation, MW, and delivery system

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
base: Multiple controlled trials; multi-weight formulation studies
caveat: Effect modest; primarily via humectant film and superficial hydration

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: Oral supplementation for skin hydration and wrinkles
base: 2025 meta-analysis of 7 RCTs; Nature Scientific Reports 2025 RCT n=150 with 1.8 MDa sodium hyaluronate
effect: Statistically significant improvement in hydration, elasticity, and wrinkle depth
caveat: Outside cosmetic topical scope; not a claim for a topical serum; effect sizes modest

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: Injectable dermal fillers (crosslinked HA, e.g. Restylane, Juvederm, Profhilo)
base: Decades of clinical use; multiple RCTs and systematic reviews
effect: Immediate volume restoration; effect lasts months
caveat: Medical device territory, not cosmetic; requires practitioner and falls outside 1223/2009 cosmetic scope

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: "Holds 1,000 times its weight in water" skin-hydration advantage
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The 1,000:1 ratio is a laboratory property of the raw polymer measured under ideal humidity, not a measurement made in skin. Human trials measured corneometry hydration, and it improves; none measured the ratio in skin, and none showed hydration scaling with it

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
outcome: "Fills wrinkles from within" needle-free filler equivalence
base: Marketing claim
caveat: Topical trials measured wrinkle depth and appearance improving through surface smoothing of hydrated upper layers. Volume restoration is documented only for crosslinked hyaluronic acid injected into the dermis, a separate medical-device category; no human study shows a topical reaching the dermis or adding volume from within

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
base: Decades of widespread cosmetic use across all molecular weights
caveat: Occasional dryness in low-humidity environments if used without occlusive layer (humectant can pull water outward when air is dry)

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
outcome: Muscle hypertrophy + hyperplasia via IGF-1R (the biology)
base: Cell culture + animal models
caveat: The biology, in a dish and in animals — not injected LR3 in humans

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
outcome: IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building in humans (the marketed use)
base: None
caveat: Zero human trials for any endpoint; it's a cell-culture reagent

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
base: Debated
caveat: Established in vitro/animal; human extent at realistic doses is disputed

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
base: FDA/EMA-approved
caveat: Native form, rare pediatric indication — not LR3, not muscle

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
base: Mecasermin record + IGF-1R pharmacology
caveat: Hypoglycemia (primary acute risk; with insulin → hospitalisations reported); tumour promotion — LR3 is engineered to evade the IGFBP system that normally reins IGF-1 in, an unstudied long-term oncogenic concern; acromegaly-like organ growth; insulin resistance; gray-market misfolding/endotoxin; WADA-banned

name: Ipamorelin
outcome: Selective GH release (no cortisol/prolactin) in humans
base: Raun 1998 + human PK studies
caveat: Selectivity + GH release are genuine; this is not the same as clinical benefit

name: Ipamorelin
outcome: Clinical efficacy (postoperative ileus — the only tested indication)
base: Phase 2 RCT (Beck 2014, n=87)
caveat: The single rigorous human trial did not work

name: Ipamorelin
outcome: Anti-aging / fat loss / muscle (the popular use)
base: None in humans
caveat: Never tested in humans for these goals — "selectivity is real, efficacy for body composition is untested"

name: Ipamorelin
base: Well tolerated in the POI trials; no long-term data
caveat: No long-term human data; GH/IGF-1 elevation carries theoretical risks; WADA-banned

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: Robust, repeatedly demonstrated in humans (IV kisspeptin → rapid LH/FSH rise); genetics confirm necessity
caveat: Well-established physiology in controlled settings; short-acting; response shows sexual dimorphism (differs by sex and menstrual-cycle phase)

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: Clinical trials: kisspeptin can trigger oocyte maturation with lower OHSS risk than standard hCG
caveat: Promising, actively developed; not yet a standard/approved trigger

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: 2 randomized placebo-controlled trials (JAMA Netw Open 2022/2023, 32 women + 32 men): modulated sexual-processing brain networks, ↑"sexy" ratings, ↓aversion
caveat: Real RCT signal on brain processing + self-report — but early, small, mechanistic endpoints; not an approved HSDD drug

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: Mechanistic + clinical studies restoring axis activity
caveat: Coherent, promising; still investigational

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: Extrapolated from LH/FSH stimulation
caveat: Not clinically established for this purpose; short half-life and pulsatility make casual self-use dubious

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
base: Controlled-setting trials
caveat: Reported well-tolerated with no significant adverse events in trials; but off-protocol self-dosing of an upstream reproductive hormone is unstudied; product quality unverified

name: Kojic Acid
base: Deo 2013 randomized study — 1% kojic acid alone reduced melasma severity, with the combination against hydroquinone stronger
caveat: Most efficacy data comes from 2–4%, above the EU 1% cap; often used in combination

name: Kojic Acid
base: Combination-serum evidence (kojic acid paired with tranexamic acid and niacinamide)
effect: Softening of post-inflammatory marks within a combination
caveat: A combination result, not kojic acid alone

name: Kojic Acid
base: Saeedi 2022 review — among the most consistent tyrosinase inhibitors in comparative in-vitro work, acting by chelating the enzyme's copper cofactor
effect: Strong, consistent enzyme inhibition in the lab
caveat: This grades the MECHANISM, not clinical proof; lab potency does not equal a cosmetic outcome

name: Kojic Acid
base: EU Regulation 2024/996 caps kojic acid at 1%; most efficacy studies used 2–4%
effect: Compliant products are milder than the studied strengths
caveat: Isolated standalone evidence at a 1% compliant strength is limited

name: Kojic Acid
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The human trials measured melasma severity and the fading of discrete spots and post-inflammatory marks — not a shift in baseline constitutive skin colour, which is what the whitening promise asserts. Evens the look of existing pigment; does not lighten the skin a person already has

name: Kojic Acid
base: Nakagawa 1995 — kojic acid is the brightener most associated with allergic contact dermatitis; it is also chemically unstable and photosensitising
effect: Higher irritation and sensitisation risk than gentler brighteners
caveat: A caution, not a benefit. Patch test; well-packaged (opaque, air-tight) products matter because it oxidises

name: KPV
base: Substantial preclinical (Dalmasso PepT1; Kannengiesser 2008 DSS/TNBS; Xiao 2017 nanoparticle)
caveat: Strong, mechanistically well-characterised animal data — but no human trials

name: KPV
base: Dalmasso et al. + PepT1-knockout confirmation
caveat: Established preclinically; not melanocortin-mediated

name: KPV
outcome: Human use (IBD, dermatitis, gut healing — the marketed use)
base: None
caveat: Zero completed human RCTs in any indication

name: KPV
base: Preclinical
caveat: Lab/preclinical

name: KPV
base: Preclinical + preliminary
caveat: No melanocortin activity (so no pigmentation/appetite/BP effects); stable; but no human safety data

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
base: Positive Phase 2b (342 pts) → but Phase 3 CeDLara discontinued for futility (2022)
caveat: The defining fact: reached Phase 3 and failed — couldn't feasibly beat placebo. Not "untested" — tested and negative at the pivotal stage

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
base: Well-characterized: zonulin antagonism; MLCK inhibition; rodent + porcine barrier-repair models
caveat: The biology of tight-junction regulation is real and elegant; it didn't translate to a positive clinical endpoint

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
outcome: "Leaky gut" / general gut health (the gray-market pitch)
base: No approved indication; concept-level only
caveat: The broad wellness claim is not supported; even the targeted celiac trial failed

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
base: Early/preclinical (porcine ischemia repair, pediatric research)
caveat: Exploratory; nothing established

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
base: Extensive trial exposure; gut-restricted (minimal systemic absorption)
caveat: Generally well-tolerated in trials (a genuine plus — its problem was efficacy, not safety); long-term chronic-modulation effects unestablished

name: Liraglutide
base: Phase 3 (LEAD programme); FDA-approved
caveat: Slightly less than semaglutide (SUSTAIN-10)

name: Liraglutide
base: SCALE Phase 3 (~8.4% at 56 wk); FDA-approved (first GLP-1 for obesity)
caveat: Less than semaglutide (~15%) / tirzepatide (~22%); daily injection; head-to-head STEP 8 = 6.4% vs 15.8%

name: Liraglutide
base: LEADER (n=9,340); FDA-approved
caveat: In T2D + established CVD only — not generalisable to non-diabetic obesity (unlike semaglutide's SELECT)

name: Liraglutide
base: Kelly 2020 (NEJM); FDA-approved
caveat: Longest pediatric track record in the class

name: Liraglutide
base: Large trials + 15+ years post-marketing
caveat: GI effects; gallbladder events (1–2%); rare pancreatitis; boxed thyroid C-cell warning (rodent); more injection-site reactions (7× the injections of a weekly drug)

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
base: Extensive: potent broad-spectrum membrane-disrupting killing; surges in wounds
caveat: This grades LL-37's natural role — NOT a demonstrated benefit of taking exogenous LL-37

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
outcome: Exogenous wound healing / antimicrobial therapy (the marketed use)
base: Preclinical / early; angiogenic & pro-healing in models
caveat: No approved product; no human trials establishing efficacy or safety of exogenous use

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
base: Strong: abnormal processing → pro-inflammatory fragments → erythema/telangiectasia
caveat: This is evidence it drives disease — a reason for caution, not a therapeutic credential

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
outcome: Role in psoriasis / lupus (as a cause)
base: Strong: LL-37+self-DNA→TLR9→break self-tolerance; T-cell autoantigen; NET/IFN in SLE
caveat: Again: robust evidence of harm/pathogenic role, underscoring the risk of exogenous use

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
base: Not established in humans
caveat: It's a pro-inflammatory alarmin; plausible to trigger/worsen inflammatory-autoimmune skin disease; contraindicated intuition for rosacea/psoriasis-prone users

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
outcome: Topical: appearance of photoaged skin (fine lines, wrinkle depth, roughness)
base: Robinson 2005 — 12-week split-face, double-blind, vehicle-controlled RCT, n=93
caveat: Best-evidenced cosmetic peptide of this set, but effect is modest; single main RCT; small

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
outcome: ECM synthesis in vitro (collagen I/III, fibronectin, HA; MMP-1↓, TIMP-1↑)
base: Fibroblast assays at ~3 ppm
caveat: In vitro; the eye-catching "+117%/+327%" figures are cell-culture, not human outcomes

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
base: Some measures vs low-dose retinol (~0.07%)
effect: Comparable on select endpoints, far gentler
caveat: Not equivalent to prescription retinoids; comparison is to low-concentration retinol

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
base: Efficacy seen at 3–5 ppm; formal dose-escalation not published
caveat: Better than plain KTTKS, but a formal 1–50 ppm dose study is lacking

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The 12-week vehicle-controlled human trial measured appearance — fine lines, wrinkle depth, roughness — not collagen synthesis in skin; the collagen mechanism itself rests on fibroblast culture

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The +117% figure is a fibroblast cell-culture readout, not a human result. The human data is a single unreplicated manufacturer-run biopsy study showing raised collagen and elastin markers over about four months of twice-daily use — the right direction, well short of the quoted magnitude

name: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4
base: Broad cosmetic use
caveat: Non-irritating; suitable for sensitive skin

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
base: Phase 3 GLORY-2 (2025): ~20.1% at / 60 wk; GLORY-1 (NEJM 2025) positive → China NMPA approval
caveat: Approved, strong efficacy — but pivotal data are Chinese-population only; not approved/validated elsewhere yet. The "A" is region-qualified

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
base: DREAMS-1/-2 (Nature, 2025) vs placebo; DREAMS-3 head-to-head superior to semaglutide → NMPA T2D approval
caveat: Approved for T2D in China; head-to-head win is notable; same population caveat

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
base: Glucagon-arm effect; reported waist-circumference, lipid, BP improvements
caveat: Consistent metabolic benefits reported; dedicated MASH-histology program less developed than Survodutide's

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
base: Phase 3 secondary endpoints
caveat: Improvements reported alongside weight/glucose; long-term CV-outcome trial not the headline here

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
base: Phase 1b→3 (GLORY/DREAMS)
caveat: GI predominant (nausea ~21–41%, diarrhea, vomiting — dose-escalation, then settle); mild-moderate; only 2.9% discontinued in GLORY-2; glucagon arm still requires glucose-balanced titration (like all GCGR/GLP-1 duals)

name: Melanotan II
base: Small human studies (Dorr 1996; Phase 1 tanning)
caveat: The effect is real, but no completed safety/efficacy trial; not approved; efficacy is not the problem — safety is

name: Melanotan II
base: Small trials (Wessells 1998)
caveat: This effect became PT-141's domain; MT-II itself unapproved

name: Melanotan II
caveat: The risk profile below makes "safe" inaccurate

name: Melanotan II
base: Case-report literature + trial AEs
caveat: Documented in case reports: priapism (sometimes needing surgery), changing/darkening moles (complicating melanoma detection), rhabdomyolysis, renal infarction, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES); plus routine nausea/flushing. Whether MT-II causes melanoma is unproven and confounded by users' extra UV-seeking — but the pigment/mole changes are a real monitoring problem

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
base: Well-characterised in cell/animal models — MGF E-peptide activates quiescent satellite cells where mature IGF-1 barely does
caveat: Genuinely distinct, reproducible preclinical biology — but not demonstrated as a human therapy

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
base: Animal / model data on the MGF→IGF-1Ea repair cascade
caveat: Very limited human data; no controlled human hypertrophy/repair trials establishing efficacy

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
base: Observation: aged muscle shows a blunted MGF response to loading vs young
caveat: Interesting rationale (maybe why older muscle repairs slower) — but human interventional data absent

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
base: PK: PEGylation extends half-life from ~minutes to ~hours/≈24 h
caveat: Solves the half-life problem mechanically, but subcutaneous kinetics not formally characterised, and unregulated PEG products vary widely batch-to-batch

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
base: Little formal human data
caveat: Injection-site pain/swelling common; no long-term data on chronically/artificially activating muscle stem cells; theoretical concerns (scar tissue, unregulated growth signalling) unquantified

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: ~30 years of trials (Chapman 1996; Svensson 1998; many)
caveat: Well-established biomarker effect — which is not the same as a clinical benefit

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: Documented across trials
caveat: A genuine, often most-noticed effect

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: Nass 2008 (Ann Intern Med, 2 yr, n=65): FFM +1.1 kg
caveat: No strength or functional improvement — the key honest limit

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: Lean-mass gain doesn't translate to function; appetite drives fat gain
caveat: The FFM gain ≠ performance; ghrelin-driven hunger causes fat gain in physique use

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: Alzheimer's (Sevigny 2008 — no effect); hip-fracture trial (Adunsky 2011) terminated early on a CHF signal
caveat: The congestive-heart-failure signal in elderly is a serious flag

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
base: Multiple trials
caveat: Insulin resistance / elevated fasting glucose (primary risk); lower-extremity edema; appetite → fat gain; modest prolactin rise; theoretical oncogenic concern (sustained IGF-1); CHF signal; WADA-banned

name: MOTS-c
outcome: Insulin sensitivity / glucose metabolism / metabolic homeostasis
base: Strong preclinical — Lee 2015 (Cell Metab) + multiple rodent studies (diet-induced obesity, T1D/T2D models)
caveat: Mechanistically coherent and multi-lab, but no human efficacy RCT

name: MOTS-c
base: Rodent studies + human observation (Reynolds 2021, Nat Commun: exercise raises muscle MOTS-c ~12-fold)
caveat: "Mimics exercise" ≠ "replaces exercise"; no human administration trial

name: MOTS-c
base: Preclinical + observational (levels decline with age; restored in aged mice by injection)
caveat: Human data is correlational, not interventional

name: MOTS-c
base: None
caveat: Human efficacy unproven; hype outruns evidence

name: MOTS-c
base: No human efficacy/safety RCT
caveat: WADA-banned; vendor guidance flags caution for cardiac arrhythmia, malignancy, pregnancy — i.e. unknown risk

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
base: Multiple RCTs: NMN/NR reliably ↑ blood NAD⁺ (~40–59% at modest doses), safely
caveat: This grades "does it raise NAD⁺" — YES. It does not mean the downstream health claims are proven

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
outcome: Physical function / muscle / strength (healthy older adults)
base: 2025 meta-analysis, 10 RCTs: no benefit
caveat: The clearest negative: marker up, function unchanged

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
base: Human trials mostly failed to reproduce animal results
caveat: Some signals; overall "iffy" per researchers

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
outcome: Specific clinical populations (prediabetes ♀, early Parkinson's, PAD)
base: Small trials with some promising results
caveat: Population-specific, preliminary, needs replication in large trials

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
base: No human lifespan data; mouse data (female-biased)
caveat: Untestable short-term; the core claim is unproven in people

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
base: Extensive human exposure
caveat: Generally well-tolerated and safe short-term (a genuine plus); long-term/high-dose and IV-specific safety less characterised; NMN regulatory status contested

name: Naringenin
base: Ruscinc et al. 2024 (Frontiers in Chemistry) — comparative study of 4 polyphenols in 0.1% gels; naringenin the only one showing significant anti-lipoperoxidation in stratum corneum (others were pro-oxidant under UV stress)
effect: Reduction of UV-induced lipid peroxidation in stratum corneum; superior to chlorogenic acid, apigenin, kaempferol
caveat: In vitro / ex vivo skin model; not a visible-outcome human RCT; independent replication with clinical endpoints still lacking

name: Naringenin
base: Yin 2016 (J Cell Mol Med) — naringenin binds ERK2 and suppresses UVB-induced MMP-1 in cells and mice; Martinez 2016 (PLOS ONE) — topical naringenin cream reduced UVB-induced skin inflammation and oxidative damage in hairless mice; Martinez 2023 (Cosmetics MDPI) — UVB and pollution protection in human skin cells
effect: Consistent anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, MMP-suppressing activity across independent labs
caveat: All independent evidence is in-vitro (cells, reconstructed epidermis) or animal (mice); no independent human RCT with visible endpoints

name: Naringenin
base: Kim 2018 (Biomedical Dermatology) — human dermal fibroblasts; naringenin activates SIRT1, blocks NF-κB, reduces MMP expression. Published 6 years before Deinde launch, no brand affiliation.
effect: Reduction of inflammatory cytokines and MMPs in fibroblast cell models
caveat: In vitro; effect in living skin harder to demonstrate; brand-affiliated claims of "15× more effective than niacinamide" derive from cell-culture single-biomarker comparison at unmatched concentrations — not head-to-head clinical

name: Naringenin
base: Deinde in-house RCT — 8 weeks, placebo-controlled, double-blind, n=30, 0.45% naringenin serum; 100% moisture/elasticity, 97% fine-line reduction reported
effect: Improvement in subject-assessed and instrumental endpoints
caveat: Small sample size; in-house / brand-run; no independent replication; "% of participants improved" does not report magnitude of change

name: Naringenin
base: Martinez 2023 — pigmented reconstructed epidermis; naringenin reduced pigmentation-related gene expression and melanin content
caveat: Not translated to clinical endpoints; other polyphenols show similar in-vitro effects that do not translate to visible in vivo results

name: Naringenin
base: Marketing claim
caveat: No human study compares this compound to niacinamide. The only human trial ran against a vehicle control, not against niacinamide, and measured moisture, elasticity and fine lines. The multiplier comes from a single inflammatory marker in cell culture at concentrations that were not matched between the two ingredients. A ratio between two ingredients requires both of them in one study; that study has never been run.

name: Naringenin
outcome: "Interrupts the root cause of skin aging" causal claim
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The one human trial — 8 weeks, placebo-controlled, double-blind, n=30 — measured moisture, elasticity and the appearance of fine lines. It did not measure inflammaging or any causal driver of aging, and the inflammaging mechanism itself rests on cultured human dermal fibroblasts. Two things remain unshown: that inflammaging is a settled single root cause of skin aging, and that this compound interrupts it in living skin. The trial measured appearance; the promise asserts cause.

name: Naringenin
base: Ruscinc 2024 HET-CAM assay — non-irritating; decades of dietary exposure (citrus consumption); no significant topical safety signals
caveat: Photostability of the molecule under UV requires appropriate formulation; grapefruit-derived versus biotech-fermented forms are chemically identical

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
outcome: Topical: hyperpigmentation (melasma, PIH, age spots) at 2–5%
base: Multiple double-blind, split-face, vehicle-controlled RCTs (Hakozaki 2002 n=18 and n=79 dose-response; Kimball 2010; 2005 sallowness split-face n=~200); recent 2025 narrative reviews confirm consistent effect
effect: Measurable reduction of melanin transfer to keratinocytes; visible skin-tone improvement over 8–12 weeks
caveat: Smaller than hydroquinone in head-to-head trials; better tolerated; typically requires 8+ weeks of twice-daily use

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Controlled trials showing measurable ceramide/free fatty acid synthesis increase and TEWL reduction; Kimball 10-week trial with 4% NAM + N-acetylglucosamine
effect: Improved stratum corneum integrity, reduced water loss
caveat: Formulation and vehicle matter; mechanism robust in vitro and in vivo

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
outcome: Fine lines and appearance of photoaging (5–6%)
base: RCT of 6% niacinamide formulation reporting ~15% fine-line reduction; JAAD split-face sallowness study
effect: Modest visible improvement in appearance of fine lines and texture
caveat: Smaller than tretinoin/retinoids; multi-active formulations make isolated effect hard to prove

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Multiple controlled trials at 2% and 4% for 2–4 weeks
effect: Measurable reduction in sebum excretion rate
caveat: Effect modest; some trials mixed; regional variability (better in Asian studies)

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Randomized trials vs clindamycin 1%; comparable efficacy on inflammatory lesions
caveat: Consumer skincare framing: use as appearance/comfort adjunct, not medicine

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Small metabolic studies; oral NAM supplementation for photoprotection
effect: Oral 500 mg BID reduced non-melanoma skin cancer incidence in ONTRAC trial; unrelated to cosmetic topical claims
caveat: Systemic dosing outside cosmetic scope; do not conflate oral clinical data with topical serum claims

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The comparison exists in humans — 2%, 5% and 10% tested on sebum and hydration — and 10% did not beat 5%. Every controlled result graded on this page sits at 2–6%. The extra strength adds flushing and tingling, not proportionate benefit.

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
base: Decades of widespread cosmetic use; controlled trials
caveat: Occasional mild flushing/tingling at higher concentrations; rare contact reactions; concentrations >10% not shown to add benefit

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
outcome: Cognitive impairment / MCI (vascular, traumatic — approved Russian use)
base: Russian RCTs (Neznamov & Teleshova 2009, vs piracetam) + EEG studies
caveat: Strongest use, but Russian-only, no Western replication; methodology varies from FDA/EMA standards

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
base: Substantial preclinical (Ostrovskaya, Gudasheva)
caveat: Animal/mechanistic; human translation is Russian-only

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
outcome: Cognitive enhancement in healthy adults (the popular use)
base: Extrapolated + anecdotal
caveat: Weaker than the cognitive-deficit use — the inverse of how it's marketed

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
base: Cellular/animal models (Jia 2011; Ostrovskaya 2014)
caveat: Preclinical

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
base: Preclinical (cPG) + some Russian data
caveat: Early

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
base: Approved drug (Russia), oral, broad use
caveat: Mild (irritability, sleep changes, headache in some); long-term / Western data limited

name: Orforglipron
base: Phase 3 ATTAIN-1 (NEJM 2025, ~11–12%); NDA filed Dec 2025
caveat: Strong Phase 3 and FDA-filed, but not yet approved; efficacy is semaglutide-level, not tirzepatide-level

name: Orforglipron
base: Phase 3 ACHIEVE programme (5 trials, superior A1C; NEJM 2025)
caveat: Phase 3 complete/near-complete; T2D filing 2026; not yet approved

name: Orforglipron
base: ATTAIN-MAINTAIN (Dec 2025)
caveat: First-of-its-kind; topline

name: Orforglipron
base: Small-molecule chemistry
effect: Cheap, scalable, no cold chain, no food/water ritual
caveat: The reason it may matter more than its weight-loss number

name: Orforglipron
base: Phase 3 (GI-predominant)
caveat: Class-typical GI effects; investigational

name: Oxytocin
outcome: Labor induction / uterotonic / postpartum hemorrhage (the approved use)
base: FDA-approved (Pitocin); decades of obstetric use; clear OTR→myometrial-contraction mechanism
caveat: Real, established medicine — but a hospital obstetric drug, nothing to do with the "bonding" uses it's sold for; misuse is dangerous

name: Oxytocin
base: Historically approved (Syntocinon nasal)
caveat: Withdrawn in US for commercial (not efficacy) reasons; still used in some countries

name: Oxytocin
outcome: Social bonding / trust / empathy (healthy people — the "love hormone" claims)
base: Strong animal data; human findings largely failed to replicate; effect sizes small
caveat: The replication-crisis core: early trust/"mind-reading" results didn't hold up; underpowered studies + publication bias

name: Oxytocin
base: Decades of RCTs; SOARS-B (large, Phase 2): no benefit; meta-analyses heterogeneous (I²≈93%)
caveat: "Down, but not out" — inconsistent, unreplicated; possible subgroup/dose signals unproven

name: Oxytocin
base: Preliminary human studies
caveat: Early, mixed; nothing established

name: Oxytocin
base: Extensive obstetric + research exposure
caveat: Obstetric IV: risk of uterine hyperstimulation, fetal distress, water intoxication at high/prolonged doses. Intranasal in trials: generally well-tolerated short-term; brain delivery uncertain

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
outcome: Topical: appearance of fine lines / firmness — Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1
base: Mechanistic (GHK matrikine, TGF-β) + Matrixyl 3000 combination studies
caveat: Standalone human RCT is thin; most human data is for the Matrixyl 3000 combo, not the peptide alone

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
base: Manufacturer studies + TSP-1/TGF-β mechanism
caveat: Manufacturer-dominated; preliminary

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
base: In-vitro (TGF-β pathway; collagen I/III, fibronectin)
caveat: In vitro; reported magnitudes are largely manufacturer-derived

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The controlled human endpoints are appearance-based — fine lines, wrinkle depth, scored firmness. Collagen synthesis was never measured in human skin; the collagen data are in-vitro fibroblast assays with largely manufacturer-derived magnitudes.

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
base: Broad cosmetic use
caveat: Good safety; no photosensitivity; suitable for sensitive skin

name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide)
outcome: Topical cosmetic serum — soothing, hydration, barrier comfort
base: Topical PDRN/polynucleotide cosmetics are humectant and film-forming; the supportable benefits are soothing, hydration, and post-procedure comfort — not the regeneration seen with the injectable.
effect: Gentle soothing, hydration, and barrier comfort
caveat: PDRN is a large DNA-fragment molecule that mostly cannot cross the intact stratum corneum, so a topical serum does not deliver the regeneration the injected form does. Benefits are real but modest and comfort-focused.

name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide)
outcome: Topical "regeneration / rejuvenation" (as marketed for salmon-DNA serums)
base: No evidence that a topical serum reproduces injected-PDRN outcomes; penetration of large polynucleotides through intact skin is doubtful.
effect: Not supported for a topical serum
caveat: The serum borrows the reputation of the injected drug. Claims of dramatic renewal from a topical PDRN overstate the evidence and ignore the penetration problem.

name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide)
outcome: Injected PDRN (clinical / aesthetic, in-office) — for context, NOT cosmetic scope
base: A well-studied regenerative compound as an INJECTED treatment — clinical evidence exists for wound healing, tissue repair, and aesthetic ("salmon DNA") procedures, where it engages adenosine A2A receptors and supplies nucleotides to cells.
effect: Genuine regenerative signalling when injected by a professional
caveat: This is a medical/aesthetic procedure administered by qualified professionals and regulated as a drug or device in many regions — it is out of cosmetic scope, carries procedural risk, and is NOT what a topical serum delivers. Listed only so the two stories are not confused.

name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide)
base: Marketing claim
