caveat: The human evidence for this molecule is for the injected form, which reaches the dermis directly and is regulated as a drug or device in some markets — a different route from the one being sold here. No human study shows a topical serum raising collagen synthesis; the topical case rests on reviews of the injected literature and on stratum-corneum permeation arguments, and a 2024 systematic review found the wider regenerative-aesthetics field lacks scientific rigour.

name: Pemvidutide
base: IMPACT Phase 2b (48 wk): significant MASH resolution + fibrosis improvement; FDA Breakthrough
caveat: Real signal + regulatory validation — but an earlier 24-wk primary-endpoint miss; Phase 3 (PERFORMA) pending

name: Pemvidutide
base: MOMENTUM Phase 2 (48 wk): ~15.6% vs 0.4% placebo
caveat: Solid — but mid-pack for the class (< survodutide's ~18.7%; ≈ mazdutide); analysts called it "non-differentiating"

name: Pemvidutide
base: Body composition: ~78% fat / ~22% lean of weight lost
caveat: Class-leading muscle sparing — arguably its best differentiator, addressing a real GLP-1-era concern

name: Pemvidutide
base: Phase 2: ↓ triglycerides, LDL, liver fat, blood pressure
caveat: Consistent secondary benefits (glucagon + weight effects)

name: Pemvidutide
base: RECLAIM (AUD) / RESTORE (ALD) trials ongoing; data 2026
caveat: Exploratory; readouts pending

name: Pemvidutide
base: Phase 2/2b exposure
caveat: Favorable tolerability reported; investigational — Phase 3/long-term pending

name: Petrelintide
base: Phase 2 ZUPREME-1 (493 pts): met primary endpoint; up to 10.7% at 42 wk vs 1.7% placebo; Phase 1b MAD ~5.3% over 6 wk
caveat: Solid Phase 2 signal — but not yet Phase 3-confirmed, and the magnitude lags incretin leaders (the market's disappointment); women responded more than men

name: Petrelintide
base: Phase 2: discontinuation 4.8% vs 4.9% placebo; vomiting < placebo; no vomiting in best arm; 98% reached maintenance dose
caveat: The genuinely standout result — placebo-like tolerability, a real potential adherence/combination advantage

name: Petrelintide
base: Designed combination-ready; Phase 2 combo planned H2 2026
caveat: Rationale strong (amylin + incretin complementary); no combination efficacy data yet

name: Petrelintide
base: Trial ongoing; readout later
caveat: Data pending

name: Petrelintide
base: Hypothesized class advantage of amylins
caveat: Plausible/claimed; not yet robustly demonstrated for petrelintide specifically

name: Petrelintide
base: Phase 2 (493) + Phase 1b
caveat: No unexpected safety signals; mostly mild GI AEs; investigational — long-term/Phase 3 safety still to come

name: Pramlintide
base: Multiple Phase 3 RCTs → FDA approval 2005; HbA1c reduction ~0.3–0.6% on top of insulin
caveat: Approved and real, but the magnitude is modest; benefit is as an add-on to insulin, not alone; requires 3×/day dosing

name: Pramlintide
base: RCTs + meta-analyses: ~1–2.3 kg loss; separate trials in non-diabetic obesity reduced caloric intake per meal
caveat: Real but small weight effect; never developed/approved as a dedicated obesity drug; dwarfed by GLP-1s and newer amylins

name: Pramlintide
base: Consistent across trials (the core mechanism)
caveat: Well-demonstrated physiologic effect; this is what it does best

name: Pramlintide
base: Small studies: modest LDL/triglyceride reductions (T2D/T1D); neutral on blood pressure
caveat: Limited data; secondary effects, not a primary indication

name: Pramlintide
base: Approval-grade dataset
caveat: Nausea most common (dose-escalated, then settles); increased hypoglycemia risk when used with insulin (a boxed concern — insulin dose must be reduced); injection burden (3×/day, separate from insulin) is the practical dealbreaker

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
base: Phase 3 RECONNECT; FDA-approved 2019
effect: Modest but significant ↑ desire / ↓ distress (FSFI effect ~0.49–0.61; NNT ~12)
caveat: Approved + RCT-backed, but the effect is modest

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
base: Phase 2 (2007 intranasal, 60% vs 27%); precursor crossover data
caveat: No completed Phase 3; the ED program was abandoned on blood-pressure grounds; not approved

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
outcome: General libido / arousal (both sexes, gray market)
base: Extrapolated
caveat: Not studied or approved for this

name: PT-141 (Bremelanotide)
base: Approved-drug label + trials
caveat: Nausea (~40% first dose), flushing, headache, transient BP increase (the reason ED development stopped), focal hyperpigmentation with repeated use; caution in hypertension / CVD

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
base: NIA ITP, Nature 2009: reproducible across 3 labs, both sexes, late-life start; most robust of any compound
caveat: The strongest lifespan data in the register — but mouse, not human

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
base: No RCT has hit a longevity endpoint; PEARL showed weekly-dosing safety
caveat: The decisive gap: mouse ≠ human; durable human functional benefit unproven

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
base: FDA-approved 1999 (transplant); LAM 2015
caveat: This is real, powerful pharmacology — the flip side of the "spare immunity" longevity goal

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
base: Well-established mechanism (mTORC1 inhibition)
caveat: Mechanistically solid; the aging payoff in humans is the unproven step

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
base: PEARL RCT (weekly): reasonably safe; AEs = mouth ulcers, glucose/lipid changes, infection risk
caveat: Not benign — a real immunosuppressant; requires clinical oversight and monitoring

name: Retatrutide
base: Phase 2 (NEJM 2023, 24.2%/48wk) + Phase 3 TRIUMPH-1 topline (28.3%/80wk)
effect: Deepest weight loss of any obesity candidate to date
caveat: Phase 3 topline is positive, but not yet approved; full peer-reviewed Phase 3 data + regulatory review pending

name: Retatrutide
base: Phase 3 TRANSCEND-T2D-1 topline (≤2.0% A1C, ~16.8% weight, 40wk)
caveat: Topline only; not approved

name: Retatrutide
outcome: Hepatic fat / energy expenditure (the glucagon arm)
base: Mechanistic + trial signals
caveat: The arm neither semaglutide nor tirzepatide has; long-term effects still being characterised

name: Retatrutide
base: Phase 2/3 (incretin-class GI AEs, mostly mild-moderate on escalation)
caveat: Long-term and glucagon-arm safety being established in Phase 3; gray-market product safety is unknown

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: 2025 network meta-analysis (Sci Rep, 23 RCTs, n=3,905) ranked retinol with OR=14.10 for fine wrinkle improvement (behind isotretinoin OR=116 and tretinoin OR=6.87 in different rank position but statistically significant). Multiple independent 12-week split-face RCTs (Draelos 2020, Zasada 2020) show measurable improvement at 0.25–0.5%.
effect: Visible reduction of fine lines, improved skin smoothness and texture over 8–12 weeks
caveat: Effect smaller than prescription tretinoin at equivalent concentrations because retinol must convert enzymatically (retinol → retinaldehyde → retinoic acid) — but tolerability is better; formulation quality (stability, delivery system) profoundly affects real-world outcomes

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
outcome: Photoaging: skin texture, roughness, dermal remodeling
base: Kligman foundational RCTs 1986; Draelos 2020 histologic analysis showed newly formed collagen and greater epidermal thickening in retinol serum subjects at 12 weeks; consistent finding across multiple systematic reviews
effect: Measurable improvement in skin texture and roughness; collagen synthesis demonstrated histologically
caveat: Requires 3+ months for visible change; sunscreen use during treatment required (photosensitivity)

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: 2025 network meta-analysis identified retinol and tretinoin as superior for hyperpigmentation among tested compounds; multiple RCTs at 0.3–0.5%
effect: Reduction in appearance of uneven pigmentation
caveat: Modest vs prescription tretinoin; combination formulations (with niacinamide, hexylresorcinol) can enhance visible effect

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: RCTs at 0.25–1.0%; documented effect on comedones and pore appearance
effect: Reduction in appearance of comedones and enlarged pores
caveat: Cosmetic scope only — acne treatment is medical territory (prescription tretinoin/adapalene/isotretinoin); do not conflate cosmetic appearance claims with acne treatment claims

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: Draelos 2020 12-week split-face n=45 comparing retinol 0.25/0.5/1.0% vs tretinoin 0.025/0.05/0.1%: parity across investigator/subject assessments and TEWL; retinol showed significant earlier smoothness improvement at week 4 (p=0.031); histologic collagen and epidermal thickening greater with retinol; older 44:1 retinol:tretinoin ratio hypothesis not supported — ~10:1 ratio provides comparable clinical effect
effect: Comparable visible improvement with better tolerability profile
caveat: Formulation-dependent; results with well-formulated retinol serums are not automatically transferable to any generic retinol product; vehicle (liquid crystal formulations, nanoformulations, encapsulation) matters as much as concentration

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: Retinol oxidizes readily on exposure to air, light, and heat; degrades to less-active forms; well-designed vehicles (airless packaging, encapsulation, antioxidant blends, liquid crystal systems, lipid nanoparticles) substantially improve stability and penetration
effect: Modern formulations achieve efficacy at lower nominal concentrations than older creams
caveat: A "1% retinol" in a poorly designed vehicle may deliver less active compound than a "0.3% retinol" in a stable, penetration-optimized formula

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
base: Decades of clinical and cosmetic use; extensive tolerability data; 2025 randomized patch test mapping soothing agents against retinol-induced irritation
effect: Well-tolerated at cosmetic concentrations, especially with gradual "retinization" (dose escalation); documented side effects at higher concentrations
caveat: Common transient effects include erythema, dryness, peeling, stinging (retinization period, typically 2–6 weeks). Pregnancy category — cosmetic use of retinol is not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding due to cumulative vitamin A exposure concerns (precautionary; EU 2024/996 mandates label warning)

name: Retinol (Vitamin A)
outcome: Systemic exposure and cumulative vitamin A risk
base: SCCS opinion (SCCS/1639/21) identified potential cumulative vitamin A exposure from combined dietary, supplement, and cosmetic sources; basis for EU Regulation 2024/996 concentration limits
effect: At restricted concentrations (≤0.3% RE face/hands, ≤0.05% RE body), cosmetic use assessed as safe
caveat: Warning label mandatory in EU: "Contains Vitamin A. Consider your daily intake before use."

name: Selank
base: Russian RCTs — anxiolytic efficacy compared to benzodiazepines (e.g. medazepam), without sedation
caveat: Strongest use, but Russian-only, no Western replication; methodology varies from FDA/EMA standards

name: Selank
base: Preclinical (rodent) + Russian mechanistic work
caveat: Animal/mechanistic; human translation is Russian-only

name: Selank
base: Extrapolated + limited data
caveat: Weaker than the anxiety use; often a downstream effect of reduced anxiety

name: Selank
base: Preclinical (cytokine regulation; antiviral fragments)
caveat: Preclinical; from its tuftsin origin

name: Selank
base: Extrapolated
caveat: Not the approved use

name: Selank
base: Approved drug (Russia), broad clinical use
caveat: No sedation, tolerance, or dependence — a genuine advantage over benzodiazepines; long-term / Western data limited

name: Semaglutide
base: SUSTAIN Phase 3; FDA-approved

name: Semaglutide
base: STEP Phase 3 (~15% subQ); OASIS (oral); FDA-approved
caveat: Effect wanes if stopped; GI tolerability

name: Semaglutide
base: SELECT (Wegovy), SOUL (oral); FDA-approved

name: Semaglutide
base: FLOW; FDA-approved (2025)
effect: Reduced eGFR decline / ESKD / CV death

name: Semaglutide
base: Large trials + post-marketing
caveat: GI effects (nausea/vomiting/diarrhea); labeled risks; requires clinical oversight

name: Semax
base: Multiple Russian RCTs; routine use added to standard stroke care
effect: Improved recovery / functional outcomes / lower mortality reported
caveat: Strongest use, but Russian-only, no Western replication; trial methodology varies from FDA/EMA standards

name: Semax
base: Consistent rodent studies (e.g. single intranasal dose ↑BDNF; Romanova 2006 ↓infarct volume)
caveat: Animal/in-vitro; does not by itself prove the human cognitive claims

name: Semax
outcome: Cognitive enhancement in healthy adults (the popular use)
base: Extrapolated; few direct RCTs
caveat: The healthy-user "nootropic" case is far weaker than the stroke case — the opposite of how it's marketed

name: Semax
base: Small Russian trials
caveat: Small, regional

name: Semax
base: Preclinical + some Russian data
caveat: Early; interestingly, the PGP fragment alone shows activity

name: Semax
base: None rigorous
caveat: Not supported by rigorous evidence in healthy people

name: Semax
base: Approved drug in Russia (intranasal), broad clinical use
caveat: Long-term / Western safety data limited

name: Sermorelin
outcome: Pediatric GH deficiency / growth failure (former approved indication)
base: FDA-approved (NDA 20-443); published pediatric RCTs
caveat: Effect smaller than direct rhGH; historical approval

name: Sermorelin
base: FDA-approved (NDA 19-863); long clinical use
effect: Reliable assessment of pituitary GH reserve
caveat: Established diagnostic role

name: Sermorelin
base: Well-characterised mechanism + studies
effect: Yes, in a pulsatile, feedback-preserving way
caveat: Works via the pituitary; self-limiting (see mechanism)

name: Sermorelin
outcome: Adult anti-aging / body composition / sleep (the popular off-label use)
base: Limited RCTs; "mid-tier" evidence
caveat: Thinner data than its pharmaceutical history implies; off-label

name: Sermorelin
base: Approved-drug safety record; self-regulating feedback
caveat: Considered among the safer GH-directed therapies; WADA-banned

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
base: Billon 2024 (DIO & ob/ob mice): ↑energy expenditure, ↑fatty-acid oxidation, ↓fat mass, ↑insulin sensitivity
caveat: Compelling mouse-only data; no human efficacy; the marketed use rests entirely on rodents

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
base: Mouse: ↑treadmill endurance; ERRα-dependent acute aerobic-exercise response; ↑oxidative fibers
caveat: Real preclinical performance signal — but animal-only, and WADA-banned for exactly this reason

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
base: Cell + mouse: upregulates DDIT4, enhances mitochondrial function
caveat: Mechanistically well-shown in models; not human-validated

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
outcome: Cardiac / other (heart failure, muscle regen, kidney/aging)
base: Mouse: cardiac protection vs pressure overload; ERRα in muscle regeneration (FASEB 2025)
caveat: Early, model-specific; exploratory

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
base: None — no human trials; poor oral bioavailability documented
caveat: The double gap: no human data and a known PK flaw that undermines oral use (hence the SLU-PP-915 successor)

name: SLU-PP-332 (ERR agonist / "exercise mimetic")
base: Short rodent studies
caveat: No human safety data; banned in sport; chronic-use effects unknown

name: SLU-PP-915
base: Mouse studies: enhanced running distance/duration; oral efficacy (first for the class); Ddit4 induction; type-IIa fiber shift
caveat: Real, notable mouse data + oral breakthrough — but no human trials at all

name: SLU-PP-915
base: Demonstrated oral activity; 7-day dosing without accumulation/tachyphylaxis
caveat: The genuine advance over #60 — in mice; says nothing about human PK/safety

name: SLU-PP-915
outcome: Metabolic syndrome / heart failure / muscle atrophy / DMD
base: Preclinical (Billon 2024; Circulation heart-failure work; aging/DMD models)
caveat: Interesting breadth, all animal/cell; none established

name: SLU-PP-915
base: None
caveat: Zero human data as of 2026

name: SLU-PP-915
base: Detection methods under development (2026)
caveat: Being built into anti-doping before approval; class is prohibited-category

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
base: Manufacturer in-vivo (Lipotec, n=17, 10%, 28 days, "~63%") + in-vitro SNARE assays
caveat: Manufacturer-dominated; no independent standalone RCT; the ~63% figure is not independently replicated

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
base: Manufacturer in-vitro/in-vivo comparison
caveat: A manufacturer claim; Argireline has the larger independent evidence base — "newer/more potent" ≠ better-evidenced

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
outcome: Reaching the neuromuscular junction at cosmetic doses
base: Penetration considerations
caveat: Same stratum-corneum barrier as Argireline

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
base: None (no head-to-head)
caveat: No trial vs botulinum toxin; mechanism is different (reversible competition) and much weaker

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
outcome: "Up to 63% reduction in wrinkle depth" headline magnitude
base: Marketing claim
caveat: Wrinkle depth was measured, but only in a single manufacturer-run periocular study of 17 subjects at 10% peptide over 28 days; no independent group has reproduced the figure, and the concentration tested is far above what finished cosmetics contain

name: Acetyl Octapeptide-3
base: Broad cosmetic use
caveat: Regarded as safe as used

name: Squalane
outcome: Emollient softening & moisture retention (appearance/feel of dry skin)
base: Well-established emollient with a long cosmetic track record; a saturated hydrocarbon closely related to squalene, a natural component of human sebum. Reviews of moisturiser ingredients class it as an emollient with light occlusive behaviour that reduces transepidermal water loss and improves the feel of dry skin.
effect: Softer, smoother-feeling skin and reduced flaking, with lightweight, non-greasy moisture; suits most skin types
caveat: This is emollient/occlusive action — sealing and softening — not structural barrier-lipid replacement. For a genuinely lipid-depleted barrier, ceramides (with cholesterol and fatty acids) address the structural deficit that squalane does not.

name: Squalane
outcome: Lightweight, non-comedogenic facial oil for oily / acne-prone skin (tolerability)
base: Widely reported as non-comedogenic and well tolerated across skin types, including oily and acne-prone, because it is a stable sebum-mimetic hydrocarbon rather than a heavy triglyceride plant oil.
effect: Lipid support and comfort without a heavy or pore-clogging feel — a practical facial-oil option for skin that cannot tolerate richer oils
caveat: Individual comedogenicity still varies; patch-test. "Non-comedogenic" is a general-tendency claim, not a guarantee for every person.

name: Squalane
base: Squalane is the hydrogenated, oxidatively stable form of squalene; some laboratory work attributes mild antioxidant or skin-protective activity to it, but clinical evidence in finished cosmetics is thin.
effect: Possible modest supportive benefit; not a primary anti-ageing active
caveat: Preliminary and largely mechanistic — squalane should not be positioned as an anti-ageing treatment or a substitute for evidenced actives (retinoids, vitamin C).

name: Squalane
base: Used in clinical and dermatology practice as a gentle, non-reactive emollient on sensitised or post-procedure skin; supported mainly by tolerability and clinical experience rather than large trials.
effect: A comfortable, non-stinging emollient layer on reactive skin
caveat: Comfort and appearance support only — not a treatment for any condition, and not a substitute for post-procedure medical guidance.

name: Squalane
base: Marketing claim
caveat: Human measurement of squalane covers moisturisation and tolerability; sebum output has never been measured in a trial. The balancing story is mechanism only — skin sensing adequate surface lipid and easing its own production — with no outcome data behind it. What was measured is how skin felt and how much water it held, not how much oil it makes.

name: Elamipretide
outcome: Barth syndrome — muscle strength (the approved use)
base: Phase 3 (TAZPOWER) crossover + open-label extension + natural-history comparison (Hornby 2022); ~48-wk gains in 6-min walk, symptom scale, cardiac stroke volume → FDA accelerated approval Sep 2025
caveat: Approved, but for an ultra-rare disease (~1 in 300,000–500,000 births); accelerated approval → confirmatory trial still owed; the randomized phase actually missed its primary endpoint — approval leaned on the extension + intermediate endpoint

name: Elamipretide
base: MMPOWER-3 Phase 3 — did NOT meet primary endpoints (6-min walk, symptom score) in the overall population
caveat: The pivotal trial for the "mito myopathy" use failed; a short earlier study (5-day) had shown a walk-distance signal that didn't hold up

name: Elamipretide
base: Multiple Phase 2 trials (Bendavia/elamipretide; Daubert 2017) — improved myocardial bioenergetics but no functional/clinical benefit
caveat: "Fixed the chemistry, didn't move the patient" — a recurring theme; long-term HFpEF trials did not translate mechanism into outcomes

name: Elamipretide
base: Phase 2 (ReCLAIM) — mixed/negative on primary endpoints
caveat: Some exploratory signals; did not establish efficacy

name: Elamipretide
base: Preclinical + early clinical
caveat: Strong animal data (kidney protection sustained months after dosing); human efficacy not established

name: Elamipretide
outcome: "Cellular energy / anti-aging / exercise capacity in healthy people" (the popular use)
base: Aged-mouse studies (Siegel 2013; Campbell 2019 — restored redox balance, improved exercise tolerance in old mice)
caveat: The headline longevity claim is animal-only; not demonstrated in healthy humans. This is what most gray-market buyers want it for — and it's the weakest-evidenced use

name: Elamipretide
base: Preclinical models only
caveat: Improves mitochondrial/synaptic health in rodent models; not confirmed in humans

name: Elamipretide
base: FDA trial program + Phase 2 across indications
caveat: Generally well tolerated; most common AE is injection-site reaction; rare serious hypersensitivity reactions possible (per FDA label); water-soluble, renally excreted

name: Survodutide (BI 456906)
base: Phase 3 SYNCHRONIZE-1 (2026): −16.6% at 76 wk vs 3.2% placebo, p<0.0001; 85.1% ≥5% loss. Phase 2 (2024): −14.9% at 46 wk
caveat: Strong, positive Phase 3 — but not yet approved (confirmatory/regulatory pending); below tirzepatide numerically (no direct comparison)

name: Survodutide (BI 456906)
base: Phase 2 MASH (Sanyal, NEJM 2024): resolution ~83% top dose vs ~18% placebo; dedicated Phase 3 LIVERAGE ongoing
caveat: Standout Phase 2 liver data (the glucagon-arm advantage); Phase 3 MASH histology outcomes still to confirm

name: Survodutide (BI 456906)
base: SYNCHRONIZE-2 (obesity + diabetes) in Phase 3
caveat: In development; glucagon component makes glucose handling a balance rather than a pure win

name: Survodutide (BI 456906)
base: SYNCHRONIZE-CVOT ongoing
caveat: Being formally tested; no outcome data yet; transient heart-rate increase is a class + glucagon consideration

name: Survodutide (BI 456906)
base: Phase 2/3 program
caveat: GI effects (nausea/diarrhea/vomiting — GLP-1 class); transient aminotransferase (liver-enzyme) elevations; small transient heart-rate rise (glucagon-mediated sympathetic activation); the missing-GIP "insulin safety net" makes titration more demanding

name: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate
outcome: Appearance of expression/dynamic wrinkles (the cosmetic use)
base: In-vitro: blocks muscle-cell contraction substantially; in-vivo cosmetic testing: visibly reduced wrinkle depth over ~28 days (36-subject studies)
caveat: Solid cosmetic evidence for a topical; effect is mild and gradual, on the look of dynamic lines — not injection-strength

name: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate
base: Well-defined: reversible ε-subunit block, from Waglerin-1 pharmacology (McArdle 1999)
caveat: Mechanism is real and specific; topical delivery to muscle is the limiting factor for magnitude

name: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate
base: Marketing claim
caveat: The claim is not baseless: the maker documents reversible mnAChR ε-subunit antagonism modelled on Waglerin-1 (McArdle 1999), substantial in-vitro block of muscle-cell contraction, and 36-subject ~28-day cosmetic testing showing reduced wrinkle depth. But it stays milder, temporary and surface-acting — how much topical peptide reaches muscle is inherently limited, and no trial has compared it head-to-head against injected botulinum toxin. Not equivalent; honest framing needed

name: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate
base: Dose-dependent DPPH radical scavenging
caveat: Minor ancillary property; not the main reason to use it

name: Dipeptide Diaminobutyroyl Benzylamide Diacetate
base: Widely used cosmetic ingredient; reversible action
caveat: Good skin compatibility at cosmetic use levels; a topical peptide — not a systemic/injectable exposure

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment)
outcome: Musculoskeletal / tendon / muscle recovery (the marketed use)
base: Animal models only
caveat: Preclinical; no completed human efficacy trial for the fragment

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment)
base: Human trials of full-length Tβ4 — RGN-259 ophthalmic Phase 2/3 (ARISE-1/2); Philp 2003 corneal
effect: Mixed; some benefit, primary endpoints not fully met
caveat: Data is on Tβ4, not TB-500; no FDA approval; orphan designation only (neurotrophic keratopathy)

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment)
base: Animal models + an early Tβ4 RCT
caveat: Full-length Tβ4; early-stage

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment)
outcome: Marketed "recovery stack" (with BPC-157) for human injury/performance
base: None
caveat: No human RCT for the injuries the stack targets

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 fragment)
base: Limited human data for the fragment; theoretical concern from pro-angiogenic activity
caveat: WADA-banned; long-term/oncologic safety not established

name: Tesamorelin
base: Phase 3 RCTs — Falutz 2007 NEJM (n=412); pooled n=806 (JCEM 2010); FDA-approved
effect: ~15–18% VAT reduction vs placebo at 26 wk (p<0.001)
caveat: The strongest evidence in the register; the effect reverses on discontinuation

name: Tesamorelin
base: RCT — Stanley 2014, JAMA
caveat: Off-label; studied specifically in the HIV population

name: Tesamorelin
outcome: Non-HIV visceral fat / general body composition / longevity (popular off-label)
base: Mechanism + observational; not RCT-anchored in these groups
caveat: Not FDA-approved for these; the approved evidence is HIV-specific

name: Tesamorelin
base: Some GHRH-administration trial data
caveat: Early; not an approved use

name: Tesamorelin
base: Phase 3 + post-marketing label
caveat: Raises IGF-1 (monitored); glucose/insulin effects; injection-site reactions, arthralgia; WADA-banned

name: Tesofensine
base: Phase 2 (Astrup, Lancet 2008): significant at all doses, ~10%/24 wk; "~2× era's drugs"
caveat: Solid Phase 2 — but not Phase 3-confirmed for approval, and the "2×" was pre-GLP-1 (incretins now exceed it)

name: Tesofensine
base: Respiration-chamber (Sjödin 2010): ↑24-h EE, ↑fat oxidation, ↓appetite
caveat: Genuinely dual-action; full human mechanism "unresolved"

name: Tesofensine
base: Four RCTs: limited efficacy
caveat: Failed to become a neuro drug — the reason it was repurposed

name: Tesofensine
base: Phase 2: heart rate +~7 bpm (p=0.0001); BP not significantly raised at 24 wk
caveat: The key caveat — HR increase (mechanistic cousin to withdrawn sibutramine); needs careful CV monitoring

name: Tesofensine
base: Dry mouth, nausea, constipation, insomnia
caveat: Stimulant-type AEs; dopaminergic component raises theoretical mood/dependence questions

name: Thymalin
base: Decades of Russian clinical use; reports of restored lymphocyte counts/function; 2.0–2.4× fewer acute respiratory infections in elderly
caveat: Real clinical tradition + some RCT/placebo work — but single-group, small, single-region, not independently replicated; unapproved in West

name: Thymalin
base: 266-person, 6–8-yr study: mortality ~2× lower (Thymalin), ~4.1× lower (Thymalin + Epithalamin); animal lifespan/tumor-reduction data
caveat: Extraordinary claims, extraordinary caveats: same group, older methodology, no independent replication; can't be taken at Western-regulatory face value

name: Thymalin
base: 2021 study: regulation of immune status, anti-"cytokine-storm" rationale
caveat: Small/recent, single-group; hypothesis-generating

name: Thymalin
base: Older Khavinson-group work
caveat: Limited, dated, unreplicated

name: Thymalin
base: ~50 years of program output
caveat: The core issue: overwhelmingly one lineage of investigators; western-standard independent confirmation absent

name: Thymalin
base: Long clinical use
caveat: Generally described as well-tolerated; bovine-derived (allergy/immunogenicity); contraindicated in active autoimmune disease, lymphoma/leukemia, immunosuppression

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: Pivotal trials; approved in ~35 countries; part of a >30-trial / >11,000-subject database
caveat: Strongest use; approved widely but not FDA-approved (US)

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: RCTs (co-administration with influenza, hep B, COVID vaccines)
caveat: Benefit clearest in immunocompromised; context-dependent

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: Multiple RCTs + systematic review/meta-analysis
caveat: Aggregate evidence re-evaluated; context-dependent

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: >40 adjuvant trials (melanoma, HCC, NSCLC); 2006 NSCLC (n=95, +21% 12-mo survival)
caveat: Adjuvant use; results mixed across cancers

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: Zhang 2020 (China); Shehadeh 2023 US pilot (hypoxemia + lymphocytopenia)
caveat: Observational/pilot; not definitive

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: Extrapolated from mechanism + age-related decline
caveat: Not proven for healthy-person immune enhancement

name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Thymalfasin / Zadaxin)
base: Millions treated; approved-drug record
caveat: Very well tolerated; minimal side effects

name: Tirzepatide
base: SURPASS Phase 3; FDA-approved
effect: Robust HbA1c reduction (superior to semaglutide in SURPASS-2)

name: Tirzepatide
base: SURMOUNT Phase 3 (~20–22%); SURMOUNT-5 head-to-head; FDA-approved
effect: Substantial weight loss, greater than semaglutide
caveat: Effect wanes if stopped; GI tolerability

name: Tirzepatide
base: SURMOUNT-OSA; FDA-approved (2024)
effect: Fewer breathing disruptions; ~½ no longer met OSA symptom threshold
caveat: First drug approved for OSA

name: Tirzepatide
base: Large trials + post-marketing
caveat: GI effects (nausea/diarrhea/constipation/vomiting); labeled risks; clinical oversight

name: Tranexamic Acid
base: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 21 trials (Zhang 2018) reporting reduced Melasma Area and Severity Index (MASI) and Melanin Index with only minor side effects; topical split-face RCT and mechanistic work (Kim 2016) support both efficacy and the plasmin-inhibition mechanism; comparable to hydroquinone in head-to-head work with fewer irritant reactions
effect: Gradual, visible evening of melasma-related pigmentation over weeks to months, with a milder tolerability profile than hydroquinone
caveat: The meta-analysis itself cautions the underlying trials are of limited methodological quality; much of the pooled evidence covers oral and intradermal routes rather than the topical cosmetic form; topical is milder than oral; not FDA-approved for melasma

name: Tranexamic Acid
outcome: Topical: post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation & general uneven tone
base: Mechanism (Maeda 2022) and dermatology reviews (Forbat 2019) support a pigment-calming action beyond melasma; direct trial evidence is strongest for melasma, with PIH and general tone benefit largely by extension
effect: Modest improvement in the appearance of dark spots and uneven tone
caveat: Less direct trial evidence than for melasma; effects are gradual and depend on rigorous daily sun protection

name: Tranexamic Acid
base: Widespread cosmetic use at 2–5%; controlled topical studies
effect: Generally well tolerated, with fewer irritant reactions than hydroquinone
caveat: Patch test and introduce gradually on sensitive skin. The ORAL form is a separate, prescription antifibrinolytic medication — out of scope for this cosmetic grade

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
base: Extensive, well-replicated basic science (VIP is a real, central hormone)
caveat: This grades the molecule's natural role, NOT any benefit of taking exogenous VIP. "VIP is important in the body" ≠ "VIP spray treats your condition"

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
outcome: CIRS / mold-illness ("capstone" nasal-spray therapy — the marketed use)
base: Open-label 20-pt trial (18+ mo), 15-pt RNA-Seq, grey-matter-volume study
caveat: Almost entirely single-investigator (Shoemaker), open-label, small, unreplicated; CIRS is a contested diagnosis. Reported safety over long use is the strongest point

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
outcome: Inflammatory / pulmonary disease (inhaled VIP; aviptadil in ARDS/COVID)
base: Legitimate research interest; aviptadil trials
caveat: Real drug-development interest — but not established/approved; results mixed/inconclusive

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
base: Association studies + strong basic biology, but no interventional human proof
caveat: No controlled human trials for these self-use claims; extrapolating from VIP's natural roles is unjustified

name: VIP (Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide)
base: Long CIRS-protocol use reported
caveat: Reported well-tolerated in the CIRS setting; but it's a potent vasoactive/immune hormone, off-protocol self-dosing is unstudied, and product quality is unverified

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
outcome: Topical L-AA: fine wrinkles / photoaging appearance (5–15%)
base: Correia 2023 systematic review (J Cosmet Dermatol) of RCTs on topical vitamin C in photoaging — all 3 topography-assessed RCTs showed significantly smoother, less wrinkled skin vs placebo. Humbert 2003 double-blind 6-month RCT of 5% vitamin C cream: global clinical score improved from 6.7 to 4.4, significant density increase and reduced deep furrows (P<0.01). Traikovich 1999 randomised double-blind trial with computer-assisted topographical analysis — significant wrinkle improvement + biopsy-confirmed collagen formation over 12 weeks.
effect: Measurable reduction in appearance of fine lines and improved skin texture over 3–6 months of consistent use
caveat: Effect smaller than prescription retinoids or higher-strength retinol; requires stable formulation (low pH, airless packaging); results compound over months, not weeks

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Extensive in-vitro and human studies; L-AA neutralises reactive oxygen species generated by UV; supplements — not replaces — sunscreen protection. Documented reduction in UV-induced erythema, DNA damage markers, lipid peroxidation.
effect: Measurable antioxidant defence in the epidermis; visible reduction in UV-induced redness in some trials
caveat: Not a sunscreen; SPF still required. Effect additive to, not substitutable for, mineral or chemical UV filters.

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Multiple RCTs including MAP vesicular delivery trial in melasma patients (Antera 3D imaging); Correia 2023 systematic review documented objective lightening measures in melasma and solar lentigines with topical vitamin C
effect: Reduction in melanin content and visible pigmentation over 8–12 weeks
caveat: Effect smaller than prescription hydroquinone; combination with niacinamide, tranexamic acid, or licorice extract enhances visible effect; SPF is essential during treatment (UV re-triggers pigmentation)

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Extensively documented — L-AA is an essential cofactor for prolyl-4-hydroxylase and lysyl hydroxylase, the enzymes that hydroxylate proline and lysine residues in procollagen (required for stable triple-helix formation). Without ascorbate, collagen cannot form correctly. Traikovich 1999 histologic biopsy evidence of new collagen formation after 12-week topical use.
effect: Enables and supports normal collagen synthesis; contributes to documented visible firmness benefits
caveat: Mechanism-level Grade A does not translate 1:1 to visible clinical effect — vehicle, stability, penetration, and duration determine real-world outcome

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Some formulations market 20% L-AA; limited comparative evidence that >15% delivers more benefit than 10-15%; higher concentrations associated with more irritation and stability challenges
effect: No consistent evidence of superior efficacy over 10–15% at appearance endpoints
caveat: Marketing claims often outrun evidence; ceiling of useful concentration appears to be around 15–20% before irritation outweighs benefit

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
outcome: Vitamin C derivatives (SAP, MAP, ascorbyl glucoside, EAA, THDA)
base: Systematic review of derivatives (J Skin Sci Cosmetol 2025); derivatives are more stable and better tolerated than L-AA but require enzymatic conversion in skin (variable efficiency); individual derivatives have different, generally smaller, clinical evidence bases than L-AA itself
effect: Variable — depends on derivative, formulation, and skin's own enzymatic capacity
caveat: Not automatically equivalent to L-AA; SAP has best-documented antioxidant/acne-support data; MAP best for sensitive skin and brightening; THDA (quasi-drug in Japan/Korea at 2–3%) best for lipophilic delivery but requires enzymatic conversion; EAA has limited independent clinical data despite marketing prominence

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Well-tolerated in most subjects at 10-15% with proper formulation; derivatives (especially MAP, SAP, ascorbyl glucoside) offer improved tolerability for sensitive skin
caveat: L-AA at low pH (<3.5) can cause stinging, transient redness on very sensitive or reactive skin; derivatives circumvent this at cost of lower nominal potency

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: L-AA oxidises rapidly on exposure to air, light, heat, and metals; requires low pH (<3.5) for effective skin penetration but low pH creates formulation and packaging constraints. Optimal packaging: airless pumps, opaque or amber, minimal headspace.
effect: A colour change from clear/light-yellow to dark orange/brown indicates significant oxidation and degradation of active
caveat: Consumers should discard L-AA serums that have darkened significantly; derivatives are far more stable but deliver active only after enzymatic conversion in skin

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
base: Formulator-level knowledge — L-AA at required low pH destabilises the GHK-Cu copper-peptide complex; free copper ions become reactive and irritating; both actives lose efficacy when combined in same product or same application step
effect: L-AA and GHK-Cu are incompatible in shared formulation and shared application step
caveat: Stable derivatives (sodium ascorbyl phosphate, ascorbyl glucoside) at higher pH avoid this incompatibility. Alternative routine strategy: L-AA in AM routine, GHK-Cu in PM — this is the standard formulator recommendation.

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
outcome: Systemic / oral vitamin C for skin outcomes
base: Oral vitamin C supplementation supports normal skin function (collagen cofactor role) but oral dose does not efficiently deliver high concentrations to skin — topical route is required for cosmetic-visible effects
effect: Adequate dietary intake supports collagen homeostasis; supraphysiological supplementation does not translate to visible topical outcomes
caveat: Outside cosmetic scope; do not conflate oral nutrition data with topical serum claims

name: Vitamin C (L-Ascorbic Acid)
outcome: "Stable vitamin C" equivalence to L-ascorbic acid
base: Marketing claim
caveat: No human trial has placed a derivative beside L-ascorbic acid at matched concentration and endpoint; the comparison the claim rests on has never been run. Human data exists for each separately, never in the same study. Conversion to L-ascorbic acid in living skin is unquantified. Stability is real; equivalence is the inference drawn from it, and that inference is untested.

name: "Wolverine" Stack (BPC-157 + TB-500)
outcome: The combination's synergy / healing (the entire selling point)
base: Zero published studies on BPC-157 + TB-500 together (human or animal); no PK, no safety data
caveat: The defining fact: the stack itself has never been studied. "Synergy" is inference from separate compounds, never tested vs either alone

name: "Wolverine" Stack (BPC-157 + TB-500)
base: Coherent: BPC-157 (angiogenesis/GF) + TB-500 (actin/cell migration) address different repair phases
caveat: Genuinely elegant reasoning — but a rationale is not evidence of synergy or safety

name: "Wolverine" Stack (BPC-157 + TB-500)
base: ~35 preclinical + 1 retrospective clinical (per 2025 review)
caveat: Animal-heavy; minimal human data — see full BPC-157 entry (#11)

name: "Wolverine" Stack (BPC-157 + TB-500)
base: No completed published RCT; parent Tβ4 has some Phase 2 data
caveat: The fragment sold isn't the parent molecule that has trial data — see #12

name: "Wolverine" Stack (BPC-157 + TB-500)
base: None
caveat: No data on additive/interactive risk; both components FDA Category 2; stacking multiplies unknowns
